# Experiment Design: Transformer-Based Support Ticket Triage

## 1. Data Strategy

The experiment uses 36 months of production support ticket data from the SaaS platform. Each ticket record includes the initial customer message, the full conversation thread (all subsequent customer and agent replies), metadata (product area, customer plan, region, account age, ticket timestamp), and the human-assigned priority tier (Low, Medium, High, Critical, or similar). Minimum corpus size of 50,000 tickets ensures sufficient representation of all priority classes, especially rare urgent cases.

Labels are defined as the priority tier assigned by human agents at ticket creation. We acknowledge that these labels reflect historical agent behavior and organizational policies that may have evolved over 36 months. To mitigate label noise, a stratified audit of 500 test set tickets will be conducted post-hoc to flag inconsistencies. Sensitivity analysis will repeat evaluation excluding flagged tickets, confirming robustness.

## 2. Split Strategy: Chronological (Temporal)

The data is split chronologically: **Training (months 1–24), Validation (months 25–30), Test (months 31–36)**. This split is essential because the deployment context involves predicting priority for truly future, unseen tickets. A random or stratified split would cause leakage — the model would train on tickets that arrive after the test window, violating temporal causality. Chronological split also exposes the model to realistic concept drift: customer language, product issues, and triage policies evolve over time. A model that performs well on future data must be robust to this drift. The 4:2:1 ratio provides sufficient validation data for hyperparameter tuning while preserving a realistic, independent test window.

## 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

**Transformer model features:**
- Full ticket thread (all messages concatenated with role markers [CUSTOMER], [AGENT])
- Metadata: product_area, customer_plan, region (encoded as special tokens or embeddings)
- Timestamp features: day-of-week and hour-of-day (cyclical encoding)
- Text is tokenized using transformer tokenizer (e.g., BERT BPE), truncated to 2048 tokens (truncation from end to preserve early signals), encoded as token IDs

**Bag-of-words baseline features:**
- Unigrams and bigrams from **initial message only** (not full thread)
- Same metadata as transformer model (product_area, customer_plan, region, timestamp)
- TF-IDF weighting on BoW features

**Preprocessing:** All transformers (tokenizer, scaler, categorical encoder) are fit on training data only and applied without refitting to validation and test. Unknown categorical values in val/test are mapped to 'unknown' token. No label-leaking features (e.g., target encoding) are constructed.

## 4. Model and Baseline

**Transformer model:** Pre-trained encoder (DistilBERT or RoBERTa) fine-tuned on training data with a classification head (softmax output, 4–5 units for priority classes). Embeddings frozen for 2 epochs, then unfrozen for 3 additional epochs. Hyperparameters tuned via grid/random search on validation set.

**Baseline:** Logistic regression on TF-IDF BoW + metadata. **Crucially, the baseline receives the same metadata as the transformer model.** This ensures that any performance gap is due to text representation (learned embeddings vs. TF-IDF) and text scope (full thread vs. initial message), not metadata advantage. Both models receive equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort (grid search on validation set).

## 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric: Weighted F1-score on the test set.** Justification: The business objective requires both high recall on urgent tickets (catch real emergencies) and high precision (avoid false escalations that overload senior support). Weighted F1 balances both across imbalanced classes. It is threshold-agnostic and operationally reproducible.

**Secondary metrics:**
- Per-class recall for Critical/High (urgency detection rate)
- Per-class precision for Critical/High (false escalation rate)
- Macro F1 (performance balance across classes)
- Confusion matrix (identify common misclassifications)
- Time-stratified performance (test set split into 2-month windows to detect concept drift)

## 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameters are tuned on the **validation set only** (months 25–30). A grid or random search with internal cross-validation (e.g., 3-fold CV within validation) is permissible. The best hyperparameters are selected based on weighted F1 on validation data. Once fixed, the model is retrained on combined training + validation data (months 1–30) using selected hyperparameters, then evaluated once on the held-out test set. The test set is never touched until final evaluation.

## 7. Controls for Confounds

- **Metadata vs. transformer benefit:** Both models receive identical metadata. Improvement is attributable to text representation and context, not metadata.
- **Ticket length:** Report performance separately for short and long tickets to verify the thread benefit is not an artifact of length.
- **Product category and plan:** Stratify metrics by product_area and customer_plan to confirm generalization.
- **Temporal drift:** Measure performance in 2-month windows within test set; detect any degradation over time.
- **Label noise:** Audit 500 test labels; rerun evaluation excluding flagged inconsistencies.

## 8. Scope of Claims

This experiment establishes whether a transformer model with full thread context outperforms BoW on initial message, measured on held-out future data under realistic drift. It does **not** prove causation, compare all possible architectures, or guarantee performance if triage policy changes dramatically.

must_not_claim.claim: The chronological split risks underrepresenting rare priority classes in the training set if their frequency changes over time.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: While class distributions may shift over time, a chronological split is required to simulate real-world deployment and prevent data leakage. The design explicitly acknowledges concept drift and includes controls (e.g., time-stratified performance) to monitor its impact. Preserving temporal integrity outweighs the risk of class imbalance, which can be managed via weighted loss or resampling if needed.

must_not_claim.claim: Using the same metadata in both models unfairly advantages the transformer model over a pure text-only baseline.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The hypothesis specifically compares systems that use full context (thread + metadata) against a baseline using only initial message + metadata. The fair comparison must hold metadata constant to isolate gains from text representation and scope. Removing metadata from both would also weaken ecological validity.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer evaluates the design and raises no substantial concerns. The defender justifies the methodological choices as appropriate for the hypothesis and domain. Final verdict: approval of the design as sound.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The design maintains temporal integrity, avoids leakage, controls for key confounds, and uses appropriate evaluation metrics aligned with business objectives.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer questions a methodological choice (e.g., chronological split, use of metadata in baseline), but the defender provides a domain-justified explanation rooted in temporal causality or confound control. Debate concludes with defended approval.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The challenger raises a plausible concern but the defender correctly invokes the necessity of temporal splitting, preprocessing isolation, or metadata parity to support valid inference in this operational setting.

difficulty_justification: This case is moderately difficult due to the nuanced balance between temporal validity and class imbalance risks, and because sound design choices (like metadata parity) could be misinterpreted as unfair advantages without proper domain reasoning.

hypothesis: A transformer-based model that jointly encodes a patient's longitudinal EHR timeline and discharge summary text will predict 30-day hospital readmission better than a tabular gradient-boosted baseline using only structured EHR features, as measured on a temporally held-out cohort.
domain: Healthcare / clinical ML for inpatient care management using hospital EHR data
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer-Based Readmission Prediction with Longitudinal EHR and Discharge Summary Text

### 1. Data Collection and Label Definition

The experiment uses 36 months of inpatient encounter records from a single large hospital system. Each encounter record contains structured EHR fields (diagnoses, procedures, labs, vitals, medications, prior utilization counts, demographics) and unstructured discharge summary text. The outcome of interest is unplanned 30-day readmission, operationally defined as any unplanned acute care encounter (ED visit or inpatient admission) occurring within 30 calendar days of the index discharge, excluding planned procedures, transfers to long-term care, and deaths. This definition aligns with CMS readmission metrics and reflects the intervention window available to post-discharge care management. Encounters initiated during the index stay and any follow-up documentation created post-discharge are explicitly excluded to prevent leakage.

### 2. Split Strategy and Temporal Justification

Data are split by discharge date into three chronological cohorts: training (months 1–24, n=24,000), validation (months 25–30, n=6,000), and held-out test (months 31–36, n=6,000). Temporal splitting is chosen because it prevents information leakage—discharge summaries, lab results, and readmission outcomes cannot flow from future months into model training. This mirrors deployment reality: the model must generalize to patients discharged in future months, not to historical patients in random order. Additionally, temporal splitting avoids the implicit assumption of data exchangeability that random splitting imposes; clinical practice and coding patterns evolve over time, and temporal splitting captures this naturally. Within each temporal cohort, encounters are stratified by service line (medical, surgical, ICU, other) to ensure equivalent case mix across splits and reduce confounding by differential service representation.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Structured features include clinical signals (primary and secondary diagnoses, high-risk procedures, medication count, recent lab values), utilization history (prior 12-month inpatient and ED encounter counts, length of stay, prior readmissions), demographics (age binned into decades, sex, insurance type), and contextual metadata (service line, discharge disposition). Unstructured discharge summaries are encoded via frozen BERT-base-uncased embeddings (768-dim). No features use post-discharge information. All preprocessing is fit on the training set and frozen before application to validation and test sets. Numeric features are z-score normalized using training-set statistics; categorical features are one-hot encoded based on training-set categories, with a sparse 'other' bin for novel categories. This prevents validation/test data from influencing the fitted transformers and avoids leakage.

### 4. Model Architecture and Baseline

The proposed model concatenates a dense projection of structured features (80–100 dim → 128 dim) and BERT-encoded discharge summary (768 dim), passes the concatenation (896 dim) through 2 transformer encoder blocks (8 heads, hidden dim 512, dropout 0.2), applies global mean pooling, and outputs a single sigmoid logit via a 2-layer dense head (512 → 128 → 1). This architecture enables the model to learn interactions between structured and textual signals.

The baseline is XGBoost trained on the same structured features, excluding discharge summaries. XGBoost is a standard, robust production baseline in healthcare. Both models undergo identical preprocessing, use the same train/val/test splits, and are tuned via grid search on the validation set optimizing for AUPRC. This setup cleanly isolates the value added by multimodal fusion (text).

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is AUPRC (Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve), computed on the test set. AUPRC is chosen because readmission is imbalanced (~15–20% prevalence); in imbalanced settings, AUROC can be misleading, while AUPRC better reflects performance on the minority class (readmitting patients) that interventions target. Operationally, the hospital cares about precision (fraction of flagged patients who actually readmit) and recall (fraction of readmitting patients caught); AUPRC captures both. Secondary metrics include AUROC (calibration sensitivity), sensitivity at 90% specificity (operationally relevant constraint), calibration slope/intercept (risk ranking quality), PPV and NPV at motivated thresholds, and stratified performance by service line (to detect whether one modality drives the gain in specific cohorts).

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set Protocol

Hyperparameters are selected via grid search on the validation set (months 25–30), optimizing AUPRC. The validation set is used once for model selection and is then frozen. The test set (months 31–36) is held completely untouched until final evaluation. After test-set evaluation, no further tuning, retraining, or threshold optimization is permitted. This ensures the test set provides an unbiased estimate of generalization to unseen future data.

### 7. Confound Controls

Three key confounds are addressed: (1) *Planned vs. unplanned readmissions*: The label definition excludes planned procedures via admission source codes; residual miscoding is assessed via manual annotation of a validation subset. (2) *Documentation style bias*: Performance is re-evaluated on encounters with below-median discharge summary length; if the gain collapses, the model overfits to text properties rather than content. (3) *Service line and prior utilization*: Baseline features include these; stratified performance analysis will reveal whether the gain is confounded by case mix. Additionally, temporal drift is monitored via model score and observed readmission rate trends over the test window. If the transformer achieves higher AUPRC than the baseline on the test set, the hypothesis is supported: joint encoding improves prediction in this cohort and time period. The experiment does not establish external generalization, causation, or cost-effectiveness.

must_not_claim.claim: The use of temporal splitting introduces bias because the training and test sets are not independently and identically distributed (i.i.d.).
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Temporal splitting is the correct choice in this domain because it prevents information leakage and reflects real-world deployment where models must predict future patients. The absence of i.i.d. is not a flaw but a realistic modeling of clinical time series, where coding practices, treatment patterns, and disease prevalence evolve over time. Assuming exchangeability via random splitting would be more unrealistic.

must_not_claim.claim: Using AUPRC as the primary metric is inappropriate because it is less common than AUROC in published literature.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: AUPRC is more appropriate than AUROC for imbalanced clinical outcomes like readmission (~15–20% prevalence) because it emphasizes performance on the positive class. AUROC can be misleadingly high in imbalanced settings, whereas AUPRC better captures the trade-off between precision and recall—key for operational risk stratification in resource-constrained care management.

must_not_claim.claim: The BERT encoder is frozen and does not undergo task-specific fine-tuning, which reduces its potential effectiveness.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Freezing BERT is a legitimate design choice to isolate the value of multimodal fusion without confounding it with domain adaptation. The hypothesis focuses on the benefit of jointly encoding EHR and text, not on BERT fine-tuning. Freezing also ensures computational reproducibility and reduces overfitting risk on moderate-sized clinical text. The baseline comparison remains fair because both models use identical data and preprocessing.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer concludes that the experimental design is sound and any concerns raised can be rebutted by valid methodological justification. The defender wins because no substantive flaws are present.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The reviewer acknowledges that temporal splitting, AUPRC as primary metric, frozen BERT encoder, and stratified cohort design are all appropriate for this hypothesis and domain. The lack of detected corruptions is consistent with a valid, well-constructed experiment.

hypothesis: For hospital readmissions in congestive heart failure patients, a temporal sequence model trained on the ordered trajectory of vitals, labs, medications, and prior utilization will predict 30-day readmission more accurately than a static feature model built from the last available encounter summary.
domain: Healthcare / hospital EHR analytics for inpatient discharge planning
task_prompt:
## Experimental Design: Temporal Sequence Model vs. Static Features for CHF Readmission Prediction

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling

The experiment uses EHR data from a single health system spanning 3–5 years of inpatient encounters. The index population consists of all adult patients (≥18 years) discharged with a primary or secondary ICD-10 diagnosis of congestive heart failure (I50.x), yielding an expected sample of 2,000–5,000 discharges. For each index discharge, we extract timestamped vital signs (heart rate, blood pressure, respiratory rate, oxygen saturation, temperature; recorded approximately every 4 hours during admission), laboratory results (electrolytes, renal function, troponin, BNP/NT-proBNP; most recent value available during or within 24 hours of discharge), medication administrations during the admission, and discharge medications. We also capture discharge date/time, admission date/time, discharge disposition, ICD-10 diagnosis and procedure codes, the prior 5-year admission history, and near-term post-discharge utilization summaries from the encounter feed.

The outcome label is defined as all-cause readmission to any inpatient bed within 30 calendar days of discharge, identified by matching discharge date to subsequent admission dates in the same EHR system. Transfers to observation units and ED-only visits are excluded. Any readmissions occurring after in-hospital death are censored. The label is determined at the end of the 30-day window and is immutable; no intermediate outcomes or post-discharge clinical events are used as sequence features.

### 2. Data Splitting Strategy

Discharges are split chronologically to prevent future information leakage. Discharges from the first 2.5 years are assigned to the training set (≈50%), discharges from year 3 to the validation set (≈25%), and discharges from years 3.5–5 to the held-out test set (≈25%). Within each split, discharges are further stratified by discharge unit (e.g., cardiac ICU, step-down unit, general medical floor) to ensure representative case mix across splits. The readmission rate is verified to be similar across all three splits; large discrepancies would indicate temporal drift requiring investigation.

Temporal splitting is essential because CHF readmission is an inherently prospective problem. Hospital protocols, patient populations, and clinician behavior evolve over time; a model trained on future data would be scientifically invalid and operationally misleading. Stratification by unit ensures that care-pathway-specific effects (e.g., differences in discharge protocols between intensive and general floors) do not systematically bias either model's performance.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

The temporal sequence model operates on ordered tuples of (timestamp, vitals_vector, labs_vector, medications_vector) extracted during the hospital admission up to 2 hours before discharge. Sequences are zero-padded to 168 hours (7 days) to accommodate variable admission lengths. Vitals are sampled as available (typically every 4 hours); if gaps exceed 6 hours, the last recorded value is forward-filled. Labs are represented by their most recent value during or within 24 hours of discharge. Medications are encoded as binary indicators (ACE inhibitor present: yes/no, etc.).

The static baseline features for both models include age, sex, prior admission count in the past 12 months, Charlson comorbidity score (derived from ICD-10 codes), length of stay (current admission), discharge disposition, and a 30-day post-discharge utilization count summarizing inpatient, observation, and ED encounters captured in the health system encounter feed. Including this utilization summary allows both models to condition on immediate instability signals in the same way, rather than forcing the temporal model alone to infer them indirectly from the hospitalization trajectory.

All preprocessing transformers (z-score standardization for vitals/labs, one-hot encoding for categorical variables, median imputation for missing values) are fit exclusively on the training set. These transformers are then applied without modification to validation and test sets. This mimics realistic deployment where future data distributions are unknown and prevents information leakage.

### 4. Model Architecture and Baseline

The temporal sequence model is a bidirectional LSTM with two stacked layers (256 hidden units per direction) or a transformer encoder (2 layers, 8 attention heads, 256 embedding dimensions). The model receives the padded sequence of vitals and labs, processes it through the recurrent or attention layers, applies global average pooling, concatenates static baseline features, and passes the result through a dense layer (64 units, ReLU) to a sigmoid output producing a probability.

The baseline is a logistic regression model trained on identical static features plus the last available values of vitals and labs (e.g., most recent heart rate, sodium, creatinine, BNP at discharge). The baseline receives equivalent tuning effort: hyperparameter search over L2 regularization (grid: 0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1.0) and class weight adjustment, with validation using the same validation set and primary metric.

This design ensures a fair comparison. Both models have access to all structured predictors included in the study design; the temporal model additionally uses the *ordering and history* of measurements. Any performance advantage of the temporal model is therefore attributable to the added temporal structure, not to better features or asymmetric tuning.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is the area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC-ROC) computed on the held-out test set. AUC-ROC is appropriate because readmission is moderately imbalanced (typically 15–30% incidence), and clinicians do not have a single fixed decision threshold; they may apply different thresholds for different intervention intensities. AUC-ROC captures discrimination across the full probability range.

Secondary metrics include: (1) Expected Calibration Error (ECE), binning predictions into deciles and comparing mean predicted probability to observed readmission rate, ensuring risk scores are operationally meaningful; (2) precision-recall curves and F1 scores at clinically relevant thresholds (e.g., 25% or 30% risk); (3) temporal stability, re-computing test AUC separately by discharge year to detect performance drift; (4) stratified AUC by discharge unit, confirming generalization across care pathways.

### 6. Model Selection and Validation

Hyperparameter tuning occurs exclusively on the training and validation sets. For each model architecture and hyperparameter configuration, validation AUC-ROC is computed after each epoch (for neural models) or after grid search (for logistic regression). The configuration achieving the highest validation AUC-ROC is retained. The validation set is not used for final reporting.

The test set is locked and accessed only once: after all hyperparameter tuning and feature engineering decisions are finalized. Both the temporal and baseline models are evaluated on the test set exactly once; test results are reported and no further tuning occurs.

### 7. Confound Control and Sensitivity Analyses

Several confounds threaten valid inference. First, both models include static proxies for baseline severity (Charlson score, prior admissions, static vitals/labs), ensuring severity signal is available to both; any residual advantage of the temporal model is beyond static severity capture. Second, length of stay (LOS) could proxy hospital discharge practices rather than patient risk; robustness is checked by re-computing test AUC for both models excluding LOS. Third, discharge disposition is post-hoc but included in both models; test results are stratified by disposition to detect if the temporal advantage is specific to certain discharge types. Fourth, irregular sampling: we conduct a sensitivity analysis comparing performance on patients with dense vital measurement (≥10 during admission) versus sparse; if the temporal model only excels with dense data, real-world applicability is limited. Fifth, temporal drift is monitored by computing test AUC separately for each discharge year.

### 8. Interpretation Scope

This experiment establishes whether temporal sequence modeling discriminates 30-day readmission risk better than static summaries *in this health system* during the study period. It does not establish causality or identify which aspects of the temporal trajectory are most predictive. It does not directly prove that sequence models identify *preventable* readmissions; confounds like LOS and disposition could mean the sequence model is simply a proxy for underlying severity or hospital processes. Results are specific to the discharge unit mix, clinical protocols, and patient population of the study hospital and may not generalize to other systems without external validation.

planted_issues.description: The static baseline features include a 30-day post-discharge utilization count (summarizing inpatient, observation, and ED encounters), which incorporates information from the same 30-day window used to define the readmission outcome. This creates target leakage because events like ED visits or observation stays used in the feature may be on the causal pathway to or correlated with the eventual readmission, and they are not available at the time of discharge when the model must make a prediction.

must_not_claim.claim: Including post-discharge utilization in the static features introduces data leakage because it uses future information.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: This concern is already valid—but only if the utilization data extend beyond the prediction window. However, in a correct design, such features would be excluded entirely. The real flaw is not the general idea but that this specific utilization count overlaps the prediction horizon, making it a direct leak of outcome-correlated signals.

must_not_claim.claim: The baseline model includes more features than the temporal model, giving it an unfair advantage.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The corrupted design claims that the utilization count is added to both models equally, aiming to preserve fairness. While the feature itself is invalid due to leakage, the intent to maintain a fair comparison structure is methodologically sound—had the feature been legitimate, this would have been appropriate.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer correctly identifies that the 30-day post-discharge utilization count is not available at prediction time and overlaps the outcome window, constituting target leakage. The defender attempts to justify it as a 'realistic signal' but fails because real-time prediction systems cannot access data from the future. The final verdict must reject the design as invalid for the stated hypothesis.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer explains that any feature derived from events occurring after discharge and within the 30-day risk window risks leaking information about the outcome, especially when those events (like ED visits) are closely associated with readmission risk. They may note that even non-readmission encounters correlate strongly with health deterioration during the outcome period.

sound_design_reference:
## Experimental Design: Temporal Sequence Model vs. Static Features for CHF Readmission Prediction

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling

The experiment uses EHR data from a single health system spanning 3–5 years of inpatient encounters. The index population consists of all adult patients (≥18 years) discharged with a primary or secondary ICD-10 diagnosis of congestive heart failure (I50.x), yielding an expected sample of 2,000–5,000 discharges. For each index discharge, we extract timestamped vital signs (heart rate, blood pressure, respiratory rate, oxygen saturation, temperature; recorded approximately every 4 hours during admission), laboratory results (electrolytes, renal function, troponin, BNP/NT-proBNP; most recent value available during or within 24 hours of discharge), medication administrations during the admission, and discharge medications. We also capture discharge date/time, admission date/time, discharge disposition, ICD-10 diagnosis and procedure codes, and the prior 5-year admission history.

The outcome label is defined as all-cause readmission to any inpatient bed within 30 calendar days of discharge, identified by matching discharge date to subsequent admission dates in the same EHR system. Transfers to observation units and ED-only visits are excluded. Any readmissions occurring after in-hospital death are censored. The label is determined at the end of the 30-day window and is immutable; no intermediate outcomes or post-discharge clinical events are used as features.

### 2. Data Splitting Strategy

Discharges are split chronologically to prevent future information leakage. Discharges from the first 2.5 years are assigned to the training set (≈50%), discharges from year 3 to the validation set (≈25%), and discharges from years 3.5–5 to the held-out test set (≈25%). Within each split, discharges are further stratified by discharge unit (e.g., cardiac ICU, step-down unit, general medical floor) to ensure representative case mix across splits. The readmission rate is verified to be similar across all three splits; large discrepancies would indicate temporal drift requiring investigation.

Temporal splitting is essential because CHF readmission is an inherently prospective problem. Hospital protocols, patient populations, and clinician behavior evolve over time; a model trained on future data would be scientifically invalid and operationally misleading. Stratification by unit ensures that care-pathway-specific effects (e.g., differences in discharge protocols between intensive and general floors) do not systematically bias either model's performance.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

The temporal sequence model operates on ordered tuples of (timestamp, vitals_vector, labs_vector, medications_vector) extracted during the hospital admission up to 2 hours before discharge. Sequences are zero-padded to 168 hours (7 days) to accommodate variable admission lengths. Vitals are sampled as available (typically every 4 hours); if gaps exceed 6 hours, the last recorded value is forward-filled. Labs are represented by their most recent value during or within 24 hours of discharge. Medications are encoded as binary indicators (ACE inhibitor present: yes/no, etc.).

The static baseline features for both models include age, sex, prior admission count in the past 12 months, Charlson comorbidity score (derived from ICD-10 codes), length of stay (current admission), and discharge disposition.

All preprocessing transformers (z-score standardization for vitals/labs, one-hot encoding for categorical variables, median imputation for missing values) are fit exclusively on the training set. These transformers are then applied without modification to validation and test sets. This mimics realistic deployment where future data distributions are unknown and prevents information leakage.

### 4. Model Architecture and Baseline

The temporal sequence model is a bidirectional LSTM with two stacked layers (256 hidden units per direction) or a transformer encoder (2 layers, 8 attention heads, 256 embedding dimensions). The model receives the padded sequence of vitals and labs, processes it through the recurrent or attention layers, applies global average pooling, concatenates static baseline features, and passes the result through a dense layer (64 units, ReLU) to a sigmoid output producing a probability.

The baseline is a logistic regression model trained on identical static features plus the last available values of vitals and labs (e.g., most recent heart rate, sodium, creatinine, BNP at discharge). The baseline receives equivalent tuning effort: hyperparameter search over L2 regularization (grid: 0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1.0) and class weight adjustment, with validation using the same validation set and primary metric.

This design ensures a fair comparison. Both models have access to all information available up to discharge; the temporal model additionally uses the *ordering and history* of measurements. Any performance advantage of the temporal model is therefore attributable to the added temporal structure, not to better features or asymmetric tuning.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is the area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC-ROC) computed on the held-out test set. AUC-ROC is appropriate because readmission is moderately imbalanced (typically 15–30% incidence), and clinicians do not have a single fixed decision threshold; they may apply different thresholds for different intervention intensities. AUC-ROC captures discrimination across the full probability range.

Secondary metrics include: (1) Expected Calibration Error (ECE), binning predictions into deciles and comparing mean predicted probability to observed readmission rate, ensuring risk scores are operationally meaningful; (2) precision-recall curves and F1 scores at clinically relevant thresholds (e.g., 25% or 30% risk); (3) temporal stability, re-computing test AUC separately by discharge year to detect performance drift; (4) stratified AUC by discharge unit, confirming generalization across care pathways.

### 6. Model Selection and Validation

Hyperparameter tuning occurs exclusively on the training and validation sets. For each model architecture and hyperparameter configuration, validation AUC-ROC is computed after each epoch (for neural models) or after grid search (for logistic regression). The configuration achieving the highest validation AUC-ROC is retained. The validation set is not used for final reporting.

The test set is locked and accessed only once: after all hyperparameter tuning and feature engineering decisions are finalized. Both the temporal and baseline models are evaluated on the test set exactly once; test results are reported and no further tuning occurs.

### 7. Confound Control and Sensitivity Analyses

Several confounds threaten valid inference. First, both models include static proxies for baseline severity (Charlson score, prior admissions, static vitals/labs), ensuring severity signal is available to both; any residual advantage of the temporal model is beyond static severity capture. Second, length of stay (LOS) could proxy hospital discharge practices rather than patient risk; robustness is checked by re-computing test AUC for both models excluding LOS. Third, discharge disposition is post-hoc but included in both models; test results are stratified by disposition to detect if the temporal advantage is specific to certain discharge types. Fourth, irregular sampling: we conduct a sensitivity analysis comparing performance on patients with dense vital measurement (≥10 during admission) versus sparse; if the temporal model only excels with dense data, real-world applicability is limited. Fifth, temporal drift is monitored by computing test AUC separately for each discharge year.

### 8. Interpretation Scope

This experiment establishes whether temporal sequence modeling discriminates 30-day readmission risk better than static summaries *in this health system* during the study period. It does not establish causality or identify which aspects of the temporal trajectory are most predictive. It does not directly prove that sequence models identify *preventable* readmissions; confounds like LOS and disposition could mean the sequence model is simply a proxy for underlying severity or hospital processes. Results are specific to the discharge unit mix, clinical protocols, and patient population of the study hospital and may not generalize to other systems without external validation.
difficulty_justification: The corruption is subtle because the leaked feature is presented as a seemingly legitimate proxy for post-discharge instability and is added symmetrically to both models, masking the methodological flaw. However, it violates the temporal integrity of prediction by incorporating data from the outcome window, which experienced reviewers must catch through domain-aware reasoning.

hypothesis: A transformer-based model that encodes the full sequence of prior patient visits, medications, and lab results will predict 30-day unplanned hospital readmission more accurately than a static feature baseline built from the most recent encounter summary and demographics, when evaluated on future admissions from the same health system.
domain: Healthcare analytics for inpatient readmission risk prediction using longitudinal EHR data from a large hospital network
task_prompt:
To test whether a transformer-based sequence encoder outperforms a static baseline in predicting 30-day unplanned readmission, the following experiment is proposed.

**1. Data Source and Label Definition**

The experiment uses 4 years of EHR data (48 months) from a large hospital network, including all inpatient and ED encounters. For each admission, we extract patient demographics (age, sex, insurance), diagnoses and procedures (ICD/CPT codes with timestamps), medications at discharge, lab results, vital signs, and discharge disposition. The outcome—30-day unplanned readmission—is defined as any unplanned inpatient or ED visit within 30 calendar days of discharge. Planned readmissions (e.g., scheduled elective procedures) are excluded using discharge disposition and scheduling flags. Outcome labels are obtained from administrative records and are available post-hoc.

**2. Temporal Split Strategy**

The data are divided chronologically to prevent leakage of future care patterns into training. The first 36 months form the training set, months 37–42 form the validation set, and months 43–48 form the held-out test set. This split is appropriate because readmission models are deployed prospectively: at the moment of discharge, only historical information is available. A random split would violate causality by allowing the model to learn from future treatment decisions and secular trends. The temporal split preserves this structure. Each admission, indexed by discharge date, appears in exactly one set. The 36-month training window supports stable estimation of rare-event statistics; 6-month validation and test windows provide cohorts of sufficient size (typically 10,000–20,000 admissions) for robust metric estimation.

**3. Features: Baseline vs. Sequence**

Two feature sets are constructed for fair comparison.

*Baseline set:* Demographics (age, sex, insurance type); most recent encounter summary (primary diagnosis, length of stay, discharge disposition); utilization counts over the prior 12 months (admissions, ED visits, outpatient visits); recency indicators (days since last admission, days since last ED visit); Comorbidity Index (Elixhauser or Charlson from diagnoses in prior 12 months); presence of abnormal recent labs. This set is deliberately rich in disease severity and recency information to control for confounds.

*Sequence set:* The full sequence of encounters in the prior 24 months, where each encounter is represented by type (inpatient/ED), date, length of stay, diagnoses, procedures, disposition, and labs. Medications active at discharge are appended as a fixed set. Sequences are padded/truncated to a maximum of 50 encounters. Both feature sets exclude the 30-day outcome.

**4. Preprocessing and Fitting**

All preprocessing transformers (scaling, encoding, imputation) are fit on the training set only and applied to validation and test sets. Categorical codes are tokenized using a vocabulary built from training data; out-of-vocabulary codes are mapped to UNK. Missing values are imputed with training-set medians. This prevents leakage and ensures the validation/test performance is realistic for future deployment.

**5. Model and Baseline**

The *model* is a transformer encoder (4–6 layers, 8 attention heads, ~256 hidden dimensions) that processes the encounter sequence. Encounters are embedded via learned lookups for type, diagnosis, procedure tokens, and linear projections of continuous fields. Positional embeddings are based on encounter sequence order. The final hidden state feeds through a 2-layer MLP to produce a readmission probability. The *baseline* is logistic regression trained on the baseline feature set with L2 regularization. Logistic regression is chosen because it is interpretable, clinically trusted, and when applied to carefully engineered features that capture severity and utilization, it provides a fair comparison that isolates the value of sequence modeling.

**6. Model Selection and Validation**

Hyperparameters (learning rate, dropout, transformer depth) are tuned via grid search on the validation set, maximizing AUROC. The selected hyperparameters are fixed. The test set is evaluated exactly once, after all tuning is complete.

**7. Primary and Secondary Metrics**

Primary metric: AUROC, chosen because readmission is a rare event (~15–25%), so AUROC threshold-agnostically measures rank discrimination. Secondary metrics include PR-AUC (sensitivity to rare-class performance), calibration (Brier score), and sensitivity/PPV at a clinically relevant threshold (e.g., top 20% flagged). These metrics serve clinical end-users who must trade off sensitivity and workload.

**8. Controls and Confounds**

The baseline feature set explicitly encodes disease severity (Comorbidity Index, abnormal labs) and utilization patterns (prior admission counts, recency). The transformer must outperform logistic regression on the same baseline features to claim that sequence structure adds value beyond severity and recency. Stratified performance checks (by disease severity, utilization quartile, age) ensure improvement is not an artifact of a specific subgroup. Temporal stability checks within validation and test sets ensure the advantage is consistent, not seasonal. If a second hospital is available, a held-out population test would assess generalization; if not, this remains a limitation.

must_not_claim.claim: The temporal split is too unbalanced, with 36 months of training and only 6 months each for validation and test — this risks poor generalization due to limited evaluation data.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The split is intentionally designed to reflect real-world deployment: future admissions must be predicted from a long history of past data. The 6-month windows provide sufficient samples (10,000–20,000 admissions) for robust metric estimation, and stratified checks ensure stability. This design prioritizes temporal fidelity over set size symmetry, which is correct for the hypothesis.

must_not_claim.claim: Using logistic regression as the baseline is unfair because it is too simple to match a transformer, making the comparison biased in favor of the proposed model.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The baseline is not simplistic — it uses rich, hand-engineered features that capture disease severity, utilization, and recency, which are the key confounds. Logistic regression is interpretable and clinically trusted, making it an ideal control. The design ensures fair comparison via identical preprocessing, hyperparameter tuning, and evaluation — thus isolating the benefit of sequence modeling, not architectural complexity.

must_not_claim.claim: AUROC is not suitable for imbalanced medical prediction tasks; PR-AUC should be the primary metric.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: While PR-AUC is sensitive to class imbalance, AUROC is appropriate here because clinicians care about ranking patients across a range of thresholds for resource allocation. AUROC summarizes discriminative ability threshold-agnostically, which aligns with operational needs. PR-AUC is correctly used as a secondary check, but AUROC remains the standard primary metric in this domain.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer acknowledges that the design is methodologically sound and contains no critical flaws. The temporal split respects causality, features are appropriately constructed to isolate sequence value, preprocessing avoids leakage, and evaluation follows proper protocol. The verdict is that the defender has met their burden of proof.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The defender correctly argues that (1) the temporal split reflects prospective deployment conditions, (2) the baseline controls for severity/utilization confounds, (3) preprocessing is fitted only on training data, and (4) AUROC is appropriate for threshold-agnostic comparison in a rare-event clinical context.

difficulty_justification: The case appears deceptively simple but requires nuanced understanding of clinical ML evaluation: distinguishing valid methodological concerns from reasonable design choices in temporal modeling, confound control, and metric selection. A reviewer must avoid over-critiquing clinically grounded decisions while still spotting real flaws — here, none exist, making correct defense critical.

hypothesis: A transformer-based semantic search model fine-tuned on historical support tickets and resolved-engineer replies will retrieve the correct internal troubleshooting article or past resolution faster and more accurately than a BM25 keyword baseline for enterprise IT helpdesk search.
domain: Enterprise IT service management / internal knowledge-base search for employee support tickets
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer-Based Semantic Search for IT Helpdesk

**Objective.** Test whether a fine-tuned transformer model retrieves correct troubleshooting articles faster and more accurately than BM25 keyword search for enterprise IT helpdesk queries, measured by whether the correct resolution appears in the top-5 results.

### 1. Data and Labeling

Collect 18–24 months of production IT service management records: ticket titles, problem descriptions, resolution notes, KB article titles and bodies, and agent-selected resolution articles. Include all query logs with timestamps and click behavior. Source data from a single enterprise or aligned department set to ensure consistent ticket taxonomy and KB structure.

Define relevance labels hierarchically: the agent-selected resolution article receives a strong positive label; articles clicked within 24 hours of ticket creation receive a moderate label; articles tagged with matching incident categories receive a weak label. A test query is scored as successful if the strong-label article ranks in the top-5 results. Mitigate popularity bias by downweighting articles appearing in the top 1% of click frequency during training; separately analyze performance on low-, medium-, and high-popularity articles at test time to distinguish genuine semantic improvement from popularity effects.

### 2. Temporal Split and Leakage Prevention

Divide data chronologically: months 1–12 for training, months 13–15 for validation, months 16–18 for test. This ordering reflects real deployment: the model is trained on historical data, tuned on recent-but-older data, and evaluated on future, unseen data. Chronological splitting prevents information leakage from later incidents (which may contain similar problem descriptions or linked articles) influencing the training set, and it tests generalization to genuinely novel tickets.

Within each split, if ticket volume is imbalanced across categories (e.g., network vs. hardware issues), apply stratified sampling to ensure each split contains representative category proportions. Do not shuffle within time windows.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Both models operate on the same inputs: concatenated ticket title + full description as the query, and KB article title + first 512 tokens of article body as the retrieval target. Text preprocessing (tokenization, lowercasing, stopword removal) is deterministic and fit on the training set only. The transformer model learns embeddings end-to-end; no hand-crafted features are engineered. Ticket metadata (category, department, timestamp) are recorded but deliberately held out of both model training and the BM25 index to avoid encoding popularity or category-specific biases.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The transformer is a pre-trained language model (e.g., RoBERTa) fine-tuned on the training set using a contrastive loss with in-batch negatives over 3–5 epochs. The model outputs embeddings; retrieval uses cosine similarity.

The baseline is BM25 with hyperparameters (k1, b) tuned via grid search on the validation set with the same tuning budget (e.g., grid over k1 ∈ [1.2, 2.0], b ∈ [0.5, 0.75]). Both models receive equivalent care and identical text inputs to ensure fair comparison.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Recall@5 — the fraction of test queries for which the agent-selected article ranks in the top-5. This directly measures operational success: agents can find the correct fix without escalation.

**Secondary metrics:** Mean Reciprocal Rank (ranking quality), Recall@10 (looser threshold), Coverage (fraction of queries with any relevant article found).

**Stratified analysis:** Report Recall@5 separately for each ticket category, popularity tier, and temporal quartile within the test set. If the transformer improves only on popular or duplicate tickets, not on rare incidents, the hypothesis is not supported.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Protocol

Hyperparameters and model checkpoints are selected on the validation set (months 13–15) using Recall@5 only. Early stopping halts training when validation Recall@5 plateaus. The test set is held-out and never inspected during development. A single final evaluation is run on the test set after all model selection is complete. No retraining after test evaluation.

### 7. Confound Controls

Four confounds are managed: **(1) Popularity bias** is controlled by downweighting high-frequency articles and analyzing performance separately by popularity tier. **(2) Duplicate tickets** are detected and removed to prevent inflated metrics. **(3) Temporal drift** is tested by quartile-stratified evaluation: if performance degrades on the newest test tickets, the model has not generalized. **(4) Rare incidents** are heldout and evaluated separately: improvement must extend to tickets with <5 training examples per category, not just common problems. 

Success requires the transformer to outperform BM25 consistently across all strata: popular and rare, recent and older, all ticket categories. Improvement only on high-popularity articles or duplicates would indicate the baseline has not been given equal tuning effort or that the model is exploiting data artifacts, not semantic understanding.

must_not_claim.claim: The baseline model may be under-tuned because the BM25 hyperparameter grid is relatively small.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The grid search covers standard, well-established ranges for k1 and b (k1 ∈ [1.2, 2.0], b ∈ [0.5, 0.75]), which is consistent with best practices in information retrieval literature and matches the tuning effort applied to the transformer (early stopping over 3–5 epochs). This ensures a fair comparison without over-investing in the baseline.

must_not_claim.claim: Excluding ticket metadata like department and category from model inputs could harm performance and constitutes a flawed design choice.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: These features are deliberately excluded from modeling to prevent the system from exploiting category-specific popularity effects rather than learning genuine semantic matches. They are preserved for post-hoc stratified analysis to verify generalization, which aligns with the confound control strategy and operational fairness goals.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer acknowledges that the design is methodologically sound, with appropriate temporal splitting, fair baseline comparison, and robust confound controls. No critical flaws are present. The defender successfully rebuts any spurious criticisms by citing domain-appropriate choices.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The critic raises concerns about tuning effort, feature usage, or metric choices that are actually justified by the domain requirements and experimental fairness principles outlined in the sound design.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The debate concludes that the transformer model’s evaluation setup validly isolates semantic retrieval improvement, with safeguards against data leakage, popularity bias, and overfitting. The final verdict approves the experiment design as proposed.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The critic questions the absence of additional metrics or alternative baselines, but the defender correctly argues that Recall@5 with stratified analysis is sufficient to validate the operational hypothesis and that adding unnecessary components would complicate evaluation without improving rigor.

Success requires the transformer to outperform BM25 consistently across all strata: popular and rare, recent and older, all ticket categories. Improvement only on high-popularity articles or duplicates would indicate the baseline has not been given equal tuning effort or that the model is exploiting data artifacts, not semantic understanding.
difficulty_justification: This case is moderately challenging because the design is robust, but reviewers may be tempted to criticize legitimate choices—such as excluding metadata from modeling or the size of the BM25 grid—as flaws, when they are in fact domain-justified safeguards against confounding.

hypothesis: A temporal sequence model trained on raw clickstream and session context will predict 7-day learner dropout from an online course platform more accurately than a baseline using aggregated engagement features, when evaluated on future cohorts from the same platform.
domain: Education technology: online learning platform learner retention prediction
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Temporal Sequence Models for Early Learner Dropout Prediction

### 1. Data and Labeling Strategy

We will use production clickstream logs spanning 18 months of course runs from the online learning platform. The dataset will include 50,000+ learner enrollments across 8–12 distinct courses, with timestamped events for video views (duration), quiz attempts (score, submission time), forum participation, assignment submissions, and session durations. We will extract this data from a read-only replica to ensure no production interference.

Labels are defined operationally: a learner is marked as "dropout" if they become inactive for 7 consecutive calendar days after day 7 of enrollment, or fail to submit any activity during weeks 2–4. Non-dropout learners maintain at least one activity per week during the first 4 weeks. Labels are assigned retrospectively at the end of each course run to ensure ground truth is observed. This definition aligns with the stakeholder's operational concern: identifying learners who are at risk of disengaging early enough to send an intervention.

### 2. Temporal Split Strategy

To avoid leakage from future behavior patterns, we employ a chronological cohort-based split, which is critical for this time-series task. The split is as follows:

- **Training set**: Learners enrolled in course runs 1–6 (months 1–12)  
- **Validation set**: Learners from course runs 7–9 (months 13–15)  
- **Test set**: Learners from course runs 10–12 (months 16–18)

Within each split, learners are ordered by enrollment date. Critically, all features are extracted from days 1–7 post-enrollment (the observation window), and dropout labels are assessed on days 8–28 (the prediction window). No information from the prediction window leaks into feature construction. This temporal structure ensures the evaluation mirrors deployment: the model operates on live learners and is assessed on future, unseen cohorts. A random split would violate this temporal ordering and allow the model to learn patterns from learners' future behavior, inflating performance estimates.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

The sequence model consumes raw event sequences: each event during days 1–7 is encoded as [timestamp, event_type, event_value, session_duration], padded to a maximum length of 200 events. Additionally, we construct aggregated engagement features: total video minutes, quiz count, submission count, max quiz score, session frequency, and time-of-day patterns.

Non-sequence features include course ID, instructor ID, prior learner completion history (if available), and cohort size. All features are derived strictly from the observation window (days 1–7). No future information is used.

To keep feature representations stable across adjacent course runs, preprocessing parameters are established once from the full set of observed cohorts before model fitting and then frozen for all experiments. Specifically, numeric scaling for continuous features, categorical encoding for course/instructor IDs, and missing-value imputation all use one consistent parameterization so that feature semantics do not drift between training, validation, and the most recent held-out cohorts.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The sequence model is an LSTM with 2 hidden layers (64 units each), dropout regularization (0.3), and binary cross-entropy loss. The input is variable-length event sequences with an embedding layer for event types and a dense projection of numeric features. The output is a sigmoid probability.

The baseline is logistic regression trained on aggregated engagement features only (no sequences). Importantly, the baseline receives equivalent tuning effort: hyperparameters (regularization strength, class weighting) are tuned on the validation set using the same metric. Both models use the same preprocessing pipeline and are evaluated under identical conditions. This ensures any performance gain comes from the sequence model's ability to capture temporal dynamics, not from differential tuning or data leakage.

A secondary baseline—a random forest on aggregated features—is also trained to rule out the possibility that simple non-linear feature interactions alone explain performance gains.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is **recall at fixed specificity (recall at 80% specificity)**, justified as follows: the stakeholder has a fixed intervention budget and alert capacity (roughly 20% of learners). They care about maximizing the proportion of truly at-risk learners caught within this budget. Accuracy and F1 are inappropriate because dropout is rare (15–25% in typical cohorts) and false positives are lower-cost than false negatives. Recall at fixed specificity directly quantifies operational utility.

Secondary metrics include:
- Area under the precision-recall curve (AUPRC): overall ranking quality across thresholds, more informative than ROC-AUC for imbalanced data.
- Recall at 90% specificity: performance under a tighter alert budget.
- Precision at 80% specificity: confirms the positive predictive value of alerts.
- Calibration metrics (Brier score, calibration plots): ensures predicted probabilities are trustworthy for decision-making.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set Policy

Hyperparameter tuning is performed on the validation set using grid or random search over learning rate, batch size, LSTM hidden size, and dropout. The model configuration with the highest validation recall at 80% specificity is frozen.

After that selection step, we will run a deployment-readiness check on the held-out test cohorts to verify that the operating threshold corresponding to 80% specificity transfers cleanly to the most recent course runs. If cohort-level prevalence or score distribution shifts make the validation-derived threshold materially unstable, we will re-anchor the threshold on the held-out cohorts and then train the final frozen model on the combined train+validation data using that operating configuration. Reporting on the held-out cohorts will include the primary metric, secondary metrics, and the same threshold-defined intervention view used for deployment planning.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

To ensure gains are not driven by course-specific pacing, instructor effects, or calendar timing, we perform stratified analysis:

1. **Cross-course validation**: Test set performance is stratified by course ID and instructor ID. The sequence model's advantage must persist across courses, not collapse in unseen courses or instructors.
2. **Domain shift test**: We hold out one course entirely and train on all others, then evaluate on the held-out course. This simulates true generalization to new courses.
3. **Cohort size sensitivity**: We examine whether the sequence model's gain correlates with cohort size or course pacing, which would suggest the model learns course structure rather than learner behavior.
4. **Feature interpretation**: SHAP values and LSTM attention weights are examined to confirm that temporal patterns in engagement (e.g., submission timing, activity sequencing) drive predictions, not just aggregate counts.

If stratified analysis reveals the sequence model's advantage collapses for new courses or relies primarily on aggregate statistics, the hypothesis is not supported. This design establishes whether temporal sequence models genuinely capture richer behavioral signals, or whether gains are confounded by course-level or cohort-level effects.

planted_issues.description: The preprocessing parameters (scaling, encoding, imputation) are fit on the full set of observed cohorts, including validation and test data, which introduces subtle leakage from future data into the training pipeline. In a temporal evaluation setting, this violates the deployment-faithful assumption: in production, the model would not have access to statistics from future learners. This can bias feature distributions and inflate performance estimates, particularly if cohort characteristics drift over time.

The held-out test cohorts are used to re-anchor the operating threshold for the primary evaluation metric (recall at 80% specificity), which contaminates the test set by incorporating it into model development. Because the primary metric is defined *at* that threshold, using test data to choose it directly biases the final performance estimate. This undermines the integrity of the evaluation and makes the results optimistic relative to true out-of-distribution deployment.

must_not_claim.claim: Using a chronological cohort-based split instead of a random split is inappropriate because it reduces sample size and statistical power.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: For temporal prediction tasks like dropout forecasting, chronological splits are methodologically required to prevent information leakage and ensure the evaluation mirrors real-world deployment. A random split would allow the model to learn from future behavior, artificially inflating performance.

must_not_claim.claim: Stratifying evaluation by course ID and instructor ID is unnecessary and overcomplicates the analysis.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Course and instructor differences are major known confounds in educational data. Testing generalization across them ensures the model captures learner behavior rather than course-specific artifacts like pacing or teaching style. Omitting this control would risk spurious conclusions.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies that preprocessing parameters were fit using the full cohort set, including validation and test data, and explains how this introduces temporal leakage by exposing the training pipeline to future statistics. A valid defense cannot be made because this violates standard time-aware evaluation practices.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer correctly identifies that preprocessing fit on future data contaminates the training process, especially for scaling and categorical encoding, which may embed future cohort characteristics.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic detects that the operating threshold for the primary metric (recall at 80% specificity) is adjusted using the held-out test cohorts, which means the test set is no longer an independent evaluation. A valid defense is impossible because using the test set to calibrate threshold decisions directly optimizes the headline metric on what should be a pristine dataset.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer explains that using test data to adjust the threshold — especially for a metric defined *at* that threshold — invalidates the evaluation by conflating development and testing.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic observes that both flaws — preprocessing leakage and test threshold adjustment — are present and compound the evaluation bias. Since the primary metric depends on both feature scaling (via score distributions) and threshold calibration, the two corruptions jointly inflate reported performance, making independent repair of one insufficient.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer connects the two issues: preprocessing leakage may shift score distributions, which motivates threshold re-anchoring, which is then tested on the same contaminated data — a cascading bias.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Additionally, we construct aggregated engagement features: total video minutes, quiz count, submission count, max quiz score, session frequency, and time-of-day patterns.

Non-sequence features include course ID, instructor ID, prior learner completion history (if available), and cohort size. All features are derived strictly from the observation window (days 1–7). No future information is used.

All preprocessing transformers (StandardScaler for numeric features, categorical encoding for course/instructor IDs, and median imputation for missing values) are fit exclusively on the training set and applied without modification to validation and test sets. This prevents validation and test statistics from biasing the feature transformation.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The sequence model is an LSTM with 2 hidden layers (64 units each), dropout regularization (0.3), and binary cross-entropy loss. The input is variable-length event sequences with an embedding layer for event types and a dense projection of numeric features. The output is a sigmoid probability.

Importantly, the baseline receives equivalent tuning effort: hyperparameters (regularization strength, class weighting) are tuned on the validation set using the same metric. Both models use the same preprocessing pipeline and are evaluated under identical conditions. This ensures any performance gain comes from the sequence model's ability to capture temporal dynamics, not from differential tuning or data leakage.

A secondary baseline—a random forest on aggregated features—is also trained to rule out the possibility that simple non-linear feature interactions alone explain performance gains.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is **recall at fixed specificity (recall at 80% specificity)**, justified as follows: the stakeholder has a fixed intervention budget and alert capacity (roughly 20% of learners). They care about maximizing the proportion of truly at-risk learners caught within this budget. Accuracy and F1 are inappropriate because dropout is rare (15–25% in typical cohorts) and false positives are lower-cost than false negatives. Recall at fixed specificity directly quantifies operational utility.

Secondary metrics include:
- Area under the precision-recall curve (AUPRC): overall ranking quality across thresholds, more informative than ROC-AUC for imbalanced data.
- Recall at 90% specificity: performance under a tighter alert budget.
- Precision at 80% specificity: confirms the positive predictive value of alerts.
- Calibration metrics (Brier score, calibration plots): ensures predicted probabilities are trustworthy for decision-making.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set Policy

Hyperparameter tuning is performed on the validation set using grid or random search over learning rate, batch size, LSTM hidden size, and dropout. The model configuration with the highest validation recall at 80% specificity is frozen. The test set is held-out and used only for final evaluation, exactly once, after all tuning decisions are locked.
