Reviewers must distinguish legitimate calibration from improper model selection.

hypothesis: For e-commerce product search, a learning-to-rank model that incorporates query-product interaction features and user click sequences will produce higher NDCG@10 than a standard BM25 + static relevance baseline on held-out search sessions.
domain: E-commerce marketplace search and discovery for a large catalog retail platform
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Learning-to-Rank vs. BM25 for E-commerce Search

### 1. Data and Labeling Strategy

This experiment uses 12 months of anonymized search logs from the production e-commerce platform. The raw data comprises queries, impression lists (ranked results shown to users), clicks, add-to-cart events, purchases, session IDs, product metadata (titles, descriptions, categories, prices), and catalog popularity signals.

Relevance labels are constructed at the session level using implicit user feedback. For each query within a session, items that were clicked receive a relevance label of 1. Additionally, items that were not clicked but were subsequently purchased within the same session also receive label 1, acknowledging that purchase intent may not always manifest in a click on the search result page. Unclicked, non-purchased items are treated as unlabeled rather than negative, because an unclicked item may be relevant but simply not displayed prominently or may have been overlooked. Sessions with zero clicks or purchases are excluded from training to avoid introducing noisy or uninformative data. This labeling approach sidesteps the core ambiguity in implicit feedback: that absence of a click does not prove irrelevance.

### 2. Split Strategy

The 12-month dataset is partitioned at the query-session level using a stratified holdout rather than by contiguous calendar blocks. The split allocates 67% of sessions to training, 16% to validation, and 17% to the held-out test set.

The stratification scheme preserves the distribution of query frequency bands, device types, and positive-label density across train, validation, and test. This is important because ranking performance can differ substantially between head and tail queries, between sparse and high-engagement sessions, and across device contexts. Keeping these subpopulations aligned across splits reduces variance in model comparison and makes the held-out evaluation more sensitive to genuine ranking improvements rather than accidental shifts in query mix.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Features are organized into three groups:

**Query-Product Static Features:** TF-IDF and BM25 similarity scores between query text and product title/description; category match indicator; price range compatibility; and catalog popularity (log-transformed click frequency computed from training data only).

**Query-Product Interaction Features:** Position-bias-adjusted click probability (weighted by inverse display position to account for higher click rates on top-ranked items); item dwell time when clicked; add-to-cart conversion rate for the item in historically similar queries.

**User Click Sequence Features:** User's historical click rank distribution; diversity of product categories in user's past clicks; recency of user activity; indicator for repeated queries (the user has searched this query before).

All preprocessing (scaling, categorical encoding, imputation) is fit on the training set only and then applied identically to validation and test sets. Query and user statistics are computed once from training data and frozen; they are not updated using validation or test interactions. This prevents data leakage and ensures that validation and test performance estimates reflect real-world conditions where historical statistics are pre-computed.

### 4. Model, Baseline, and Fair Comparison

The proposed model is a learning-to-rank ranker (LambdaMART or LightGBM in rank mode) trained to optimize NDCG@10 on the training set. The model learns pairwise preferences: for each query-session, positive items (clicked or purchased) are ranked above unlabeled items. During training, the loss function directly optimizes NDCG@10 on the training data.

The baseline is a combined BM25 + static popularity ranker. Items are ranked by a weighted linear combination of BM25 score (computed from query and product text) and catalog popularity (log click frequency from training data). The weights are tuned via grid search on the validation set to maximize validation MRR. This baseline uses only text matching and pre-computed popularity; it does not use query-product interaction features or user sequence signals.

The baseline is fair because: (a) it receives the same validation-set tuning effort as the learning-to-rank model; (b) it uses the same training data and label definitions; (c) it is evaluated under identical conditions on the test set; (d) it represents the realistic null hypothesis (simple text and popularity ranking, the current production system). Any improvement by the learning-to-rank model over this baseline would demonstrate that learned interactions and user sequences add predictive value beyond text and popularity alone.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary Metric: Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR)** — MRR is the primary metric because it measures how quickly the first engaged item appears in the ranked list, which is especially informative in search sessions where a user often acts after encountering one sufficiently relevant product. In this dataset, many sessions provide only a small number of explicit positive interactions, so a first-hit metric gives a stable query-level comparison for model selection and final evaluation. The hypothesis test is: does the learning-to-rank model achieve statistically significantly higher test MRR than the baseline (e.g., >2% absolute improvement, p<0.05)?

**Secondary Metrics:**
- **NDCG@3 and NDCG@5:** Ranking quality at shorter depths, reflecting immediate visibility and the steepest user attention drop-off.
- **NDCG@10:** Broader top-of-page ranking quality beyond the first engaged result.
- **Click-Through Rate (CTR):** Engagement metric on the test set (acknowledging position bias in interpretation).
- **Precision@10 and Recall@10:** Coverage and precision of relevant items in the top 10.
- **Per-Segment Breakdown:** MRR stratified by (1) query frequency (head queries >100 historical occurrences vs. tail <100) and (2) item novelty (products in top 10% catalog popularity vs. below-median). This reveals whether improvements are driven by confounds.

### 6. Model Selection and Validation

Hyperparameter tuning occurs on the validation set. For each candidate configuration (tree depth, learning rate, regularization), the learning-to-rank model and baseline are trained and their MRR on validation queries is computed. The configuration yielding the highest validation MRR is selected for final test evaluation.

The test set is used exactly once, after all tuning is complete. Final metrics are computed on test data, and statistical significance is assessed using a paired t-test over per-query reciprocal-rank scores or bootstrap confidence intervals on the mean difference. The test set is never revisited.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

Two known confounds are explicitly addressed:

**Popularity Bias:** The baseline (BM25 + popularity) could improve relative to a random ranker simply by learning to rank popular items higher, not by modeling relevance. To detect this, secondary metrics report MRR separately for head vs. tail queries and popular vs. novel items. If the learning-to-rank model's gain over baseline is concentrated only on head queries or popular items, this suggests it is exploiting popularity bias rather than learning genuine interactions. Conversely, if gains are consistent or larger on tail/novel items, this supports the hypothesis that the model learns query-specific relevance.

**Position Bias in Clicks:** Unclicked items near the top of rankings may be highly relevant but overlooked; clicked items near the bottom may be low-quality but clicked out of desperation. Both models are trained on the same graded relevance labels (clicked or purchased), so position bias in the underlying data affects both equally. However, the learning-to-rank model, with access to position-adjusted interaction features and user sequences, may better disambiguate position bias from true relevance. Secondary analysis splits results by position cohorts to examine whether the model generalizes across ranks.

### 8. Scope and Claims

This experiment would establish whether a learning-to-rank model with query-product interaction and user click sequences produces higher MRR than BM25 + static popularity on a held-out test set of recent search sessions. It does not establish: (a) whether the improvement is statistically significant in production (A/B testing on live traffic is required for causal claims); (b) whether the model is cost-effective to deploy (latency, compute requirements must be evaluated separately); (c) causality (the experiment is observational; feature importance and ablation studies would be needed to identify which feature groups drive improvement). The stratified split ensures the result reflects ranking behavior across the full mix of query and session types observed in the platform, rather than being dominated by any single segment.

planted_issues.description: The experiment uses a stratified random split over query sessions instead of a temporal split. Because search behavior evolves over time due to seasonality, promotions, and catalog changes, random mixing leaks future behavioral patterns into the training data. This inflates performance estimates unrealistically and invalidates the assumption that the model will generalize to future user behavior, which is the intended deployment scenario.

The primary evaluation metric is MRR, but the learning-to-rank model is trained to optimize NDCG@10. MRR only considers the first relevant item, while NDCG@10 rewards improvements in the full ranking order up to position 10. This misalignment means the evaluation does not properly reflect the objective the model was optimized for, undermining the validity of the hypothesis test about improved ranking quality.

must_not_claim.claim: The stratified split is inappropriate because it doesn't preserve chronological order.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Stratified splits are often good practice to balance class distributions, and in stationary settings this would be sound. Here, the real flaw is temporal leakage, not the use of stratification per se. A reviewer criticizing stratification without reasoning about time-based non-stationarity misunderstands the root cause.

must_not_claim.claim: Using MRR as a metric is invalid because it’s not a standard ranking metric.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: MRR is a legitimate and widely used ranking metric, especially in settings where users focus on the first result. The issue is not MRR itself, but its misalignment with the model’s training objective (NDCG@10) and the broader ranking utility goal defined in the hypothesis.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic demonstrates that the random temporal split allows future data to contaminate training, breaking the real-world generalization assumption. The defender cannot reasonably justify this given the domain’s known seasonality and non-stationarity. The verdict must conclude that the evaluation is invalid without a chronological split.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic correctly identifies that user behavior and product relevance change over time, and that the current split invalidates future generalization claims.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic establishes that the model is trained to optimize NDCG@10 but evaluated on MRR, which prioritizes different ranking behaviors. The defender cannot claim alignment between training and evaluation objectives. The verdict must acknowledge that the primary evaluation metric does not match the model’s optimization goal.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic explains how NDCG@10 and MRR differ in their treatment of ranked lists and why this matters for measuring holistic ranking improvement.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Learning-to-Rank vs. BM25 for E-commerce Search

### 1. Data and Labeling Strategy

This experiment uses 12 months of anonymized search logs from the production e-commerce platform. The raw data comprises queries, impression lists (ranked results shown to users), clicks, add-to-cart events, purchases, session IDs, product metadata (titles, descriptions, categories, prices), and catalog popularity signals.

Relevance labels are constructed at the session level using implicit user feedback. For each query within a session, items that were clicked receive a relevance label of 1. Additionally, items that were not clicked but were subsequently purchased within the same session also receive label 1, acknowledging that purchase intent may not always manifest in a click on the search result page. Unclicked, non-purchased items are treated as unlabeled rather than negative, because an unclicked item may be relevant but simply not displayed prominently or may have been overlooked. Sessions with zero clicks or purchases are excluded from training to avoid introducing noisy or uninformative data. This labeling approach sidesteps the core ambiguity in implicit feedback: that absence of a click does not prove irrelevance.

### 2. Temporal Split Strategy

The 12-month dataset is divided chronologically rather than randomly. Months 1–8 (67%) form the training set, months 9–10 (16%) form the validation set, and months 11–12 (17%) form the held-out test set.

This temporal split is essential for three reasons. First, search behavior is non-stationary: seasonality, promotional campaigns, and catalog changes cause relevance patterns to drift over time. Random shuffling would mix future behavioral patterns into the training window, inflating performance estimates in unrealistic ways. Second, the deployment scenario is real-time ranking of live queries; the model must generalize to future, unseen data distribution, not interpolate within a static snapshot. Third, random splitting would cause implicit leakage: a model trained on future user interactions might exploit transient popularity trends that do not predict current-user behavior, producing a model that fails in production.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Features are organized into three groups:

**Query-Product Static Features:** TF-IDF and BM25 similarity scores between query text and product title/description; category match indicator; price range compatibility; and catalog popularity (log-transformed click frequency computed from training data only).

**Query-Product Interaction Features:** Position-bias-adjusted click probability (weighted by inverse display position to account for higher click rates on top-ranked items); item dwell time when clicked; add-to-cart conversion rate for the item in historically similar queries.

**User Click Sequence Features:** User's historical click rank distribution; diversity of product categories in user's past clicks; recency of user activity; indicator for repeated queries (the user has searched this query before).

All preprocessing (scaling, categorical encoding, imputation) is fit on the training set only and then applied identically to validation and test sets. Query and user statistics are computed once from training data and frozen; they are not updated using validation or test interactions. This prevents data leakage and ensures that validation and test performance estimates reflect real-world conditions where historical statistics are pre-computed.

### 4. Model, Baseline, and Fair Comparison

The proposed model is a learning-to-rank ranker (LambdaMART or LightGBM in rank mode) trained to optimize NDCG@10 on the training set. The model learns pairwise preferences: for each query-session, positive items (clicked or purchased) are ranked above unlabeled items. During training, the loss function directly optimizes NDCG@10 on the training data.

The baseline is a combined BM25 + static popularity ranker. Items are ranked by a weighted linear combination of BM25 score (computed from query and product text) and catalog popularity (log click frequency from training data). The weights are tuned via grid search on the validation set to maximize validation NDCG@10. This baseline uses only text matching and pre-computed popularity; it does not use query-product interaction features or user sequence signals.

The baseline is fair because: (a) it receives the same validation-set tuning effort as the learning-to-rank model; (b) it uses the same training data and label definitions; (c) it is evaluated under identical conditions on the test set; (d) it represents the realistic null hypothesis (simple text and popularity ranking, the current production system). Any improvement by the learning-to-rank model over this baseline would demonstrate that learned interactions and user sequences add predictive value beyond text and popularity alone.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary Metric: NDCG@10** — The normalized discounted cumulative gain at rank 10 is the primary metric because it directly measures ranking quality at the depth users typically examine, penalizes misranking relevant items (lower rank = lower discount), and naturally handles graded relevance. NDCG is normalized to [0, 1], enabling fair cross-query aggregation. The hypothesis test is: does the learning-to-rank model achieve statistically significantly higher test NDCG@10 than the baseline (e.g., >2% absolute improvement, p<0.05)?

**Secondary Metrics:**
- **NDCG@3 and NDCG@5:** Ranking quality at shorter depths, reflecting immediate visibility and the steepest user attention drop-off.
- **Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR):** Positional metric for the first relevant item, useful when users are searching for a specific product.
- **Click-Through Rate (CTR):** Engagement metric on the test set (acknowledging position bias in interpretation).
- **Precision@10 and Recall@10:** Coverage and precision of relevant items in the top 10.
- **Per-Segment Breakdown:** NDCG@10 stratified by (1) query frequency (head queries >100 historical occurrences vs. tail <100) and (2) item novelty (products in top 10% catalog popularity vs. below-median). This reveals whether improvements are driven by confounds.

### 6. Model Selection and Validation

Hyperparameter tuning occurs on the validation set (months 9–10). For each candidate configuration (tree depth, learning rate, regularization), the learning-to-rank model and baseline are trained and their NDCG@10 on validation queries is computed. The configuration yielding the highest validation NDCG@10 is selected for final test evaluation.

The test set (months 11–12) is used exactly once, after all tuning is complete. Final metrics are computed on test data, and statistical significance is assessed using a paired t-test over per-query NDCG@10 scores or bootstrap confidence intervals on the mean difference. The test set is never revisited.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

Two known confounds are explicitly addressed:

**Popularity Bias:** The baseline (BM25 + popularity) could improve relative to a random ranker simply by learning to rank popular items higher, not by modeling relevance. To detect this, secondary metrics report NDCG@10 separately for head vs. tail queries and popular vs. novel items. If the learning-to-rank model's gain over baseline is concentrated only on head queries or popular items, this suggests it is exploiting popularity bias rather than learning genuine interactions. Conversely, if gains are consistent or larger on tail/novel items, this supports the hypothesis that the model learns query-specific relevance.

**Position Bias in Clicks:** Unclicked items near the top of rankings may be highly relevant but overlooked; clicked items near the bottom may be low-quality but clicked out of desperation. Both models are trained on the same graded relevance labels (clicked or purchased), so position bias in the underlying data affects both equally. However, the learning-to-rank model, with access to position-adjusted interaction features and user sequences, may better disambiguate position bias from true relevance. Secondary analysis splits results by position cohorts to examine whether the model generalizes across ranks.

### 8. Scope and Claims

This experiment would establish whether a learning-to-rank model with query-product interaction and user click sequences produces higher NDCG@10 than BM25 + static popularity on a held-out test set of recent search sessions. It does not establish: (a) whether the improvement is statistically significant in production (A/B testing on live traffic is required for causal claims); (b) whether the model is cost-effective to deploy (latency, compute requirements must be evaluated separately); (c) causality (the experiment is observational; feature importance and ablation studies would be needed to identify which feature groups drive improvement). The temporal split ensures the result reflects generalization to future search behavior, not overfitting to historical patterns.
difficulty_justification: This case is appropriately difficult because both flaws are subtle and domain-specific: temporal leakage requires understanding non-stationarity in search behavior, and metric mismatch hinges on recognizing the misalignment between training objective and evaluation metric in ranking systems.

hypothesis: A two-tower transformer retrieval model that encodes the full query text and product metadata will rank relevant e-commerce search results more accurately than a traditional BM25-plus-handcrafted-features ranker, as measured by offline nDCG on held-out search sessions from a large marketplace.
domain: E-commerce marketplace search for consumer products in a multi-category online retail platform
task_prompt:
# Experiment Design: Two-Tower Transformer Retrieval vs. BM25 Ranker

## Objective
Test whether a two-tower transformer model that jointly encodes query text and product metadata produces significantly higher relevance rankings (measured by offline nDCG@10) than a traditional BM25-plus-features baseline on e-commerce search in a large marketplace.

## Data Strategy

The experiment uses 12 months of production search session logs, including query text, impressions (candidate sets ranked for each query), click events, add-to-cart, purchase events, and product metadata (title, category, price, availability, seller rating, inventory level). Data is partitioned into three chronological, non-overlapping windows:
- **Training (months 1–8)**: ~60% of data, used to fit all models and preprocessing pipelines.
- **Validation (months 9–10)**: ~20% of data, used for hyperparameter tuning and model selection via nDCG@10.
- **Test (months 11–12)**: ~20% of data, held-out and evaluated exactly once at the end.

This temporal split is critical because e-commerce search is non-stationary: catalog inventory, prices, promotions, and seasonal demand shift over time. A random split would leak future product availability and trend information into training, artificially inflating performance and masking real-world degradation. The chronological split mirrors the deployment scenario in which the model must rank products in an unseen future period using only historical knowledge.

## Label Definition and Bias Mitigation

Relevance labels are derived from user interactions using a graded scheme:
- **Label 3**: Purchase within session (highly relevant).
- **Label 2**: Add-to-cart (relevant).
- **Label 1**: Click without add-to-cart (weakly relevant).
- **Label 0**: Impression without click (not relevant).

Only items appearing in the impression set (candidate pool actually shown) are labeled; items not impressed are excluded. To mitigate position bias, only top-10 interactions are used as strong training signals; nDCG evaluation inherently down-weights position effects via log-discount weighting.

## Features and Preprocessing

All preprocessing transformers (tokenization, scaling, vocabulary fitting) are fit on the training set only and applied uniformly to validation and test sets, preventing future information leakage.

**Query-side features**: Query text (transformer tokens), query length, query-inferred category intent (derived from training-set query-to-category click distribution).

**Product-side features**: Product title (transformer tokens), category (one-hot encoded), price (z-score normalized on training set), availability (binary, recorded at impression time), seller rating (normalized), popularity prior (log of training-set clicks, capped at p95).

No feature encodes the label or future information; all signals are observable at impression time.

## Model and Baseline

**Proposed Model**: Two-tower transformer retrieval architecture with (1) BERT-style query encoder (12 layers, 768 hidden dims) → 256-d query embedding; (2) identical product encoder → 256-d product embedding; (3) cosine similarity ranking head with optional learned re-ranking. Trained with contrastive loss (InfoNCE) with hard negative mining. Training uses up to 20 epochs with early stopping on validation nDCG@10.

**Baseline**: BM25-plus-handcrafted-features ranker. BM25 retrieves candidates from product titles; a linear model (logistic regression or LambdaMART) combines BM25 score with hand-engineered features (query-category match, availability, popularity, seller rating, price similarity). Both BM25 and linear model hyperparameters are tuned on the validation set via grid search, receiving equivalent tuning effort as the transformer. The baseline uses the same label definition, training data, and feature set (availability, popularity), ensuring a fair comparison.

## Evaluation Metrics

**Primary**: **nDCG@10** on the test set. nDCG handles graded labels and position bias, aligns with the business goal (surface satisfying products early), and is the standard IR benchmark.

**Secondary metrics**:
- **nDCG@5**: top-of-ranking accuracy.
- **Recall@100**: coverage of relevant items; detects long-tail degradation.
- **Precision@10**: false positive rate.
- **nDCG@10 stratified by category and query type**: ensures improvements generalize across all product/query segments, not concentrated in high-volume categories.
- **Post-hoc position-debiased CTR**: inverse propensity weighting to verify improvements hold under bias correction.

## Model Selection and Validation

Hyperparameters for both transformer and baseline are tuned on the validation set (months 9–10) using the primary metric (nDCG@10). No information from the test set is used. Once hyperparameters are finalized, the test set is evaluated exactly once, and results are reported with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals.

## Confound Controls

1. **Inventory churn**: All features are fit on training data; the model cannot exploit product-age signals. Per-category and product-age-stratified secondary analyses verify improvements are not driven by freshness bias.
2. **Seasonal shifts**: Chronological split inherently controls this.
3. **Position bias**: nDCG applies position discounting; post-hoc debiased CTR analysis verifies the finding.
4. **Popularity bias**: Popularity feature is available to both models; secondary metrics (Recall@100, per-category nDCG) check for over-reliance.
5. **Query normalization**: Both models use identical preprocessing.
6. **Exposure bias in labels**: Only impressed items are evaluated; post-hoc manual relevance assessment (500 queries) estimates label quality.

## Scope and Interpretation

This experiment establishes whether the two-tower transformer produces better offline rankings than BM25 on a held-out test period. It does **not** establish online impact (CTR, conversion) without A/B testing; offline metrics are predictive but imperfect. The test set is specific to months 11–12 and one marketplace; generalization to other geographies or time periods requires separate evaluation.

must_not_claim.claim: The chronological data split (months 1–8 train, 9–10 val, 11–12 test) introduces bias or unnecessarily reduces data available for training.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The temporal split is appropriate and necessary: e-commerce search data exhibits strong non-stationarity due to shifting inventory, prices, promotions, and seasonal trends. A random split would allow future data to leak into training, inflating performance estimates. The chronological split realistically simulates deployment to future periods and is thus methodologically sound.

must_not_claim.claim: Using popularity (log-clicks from training set) as a feature gives the transformer model unfair advantage or leaks label information.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Popularity is a legitimate signal observable at impression time and is included in *both* models. Using it ensures a fair comparison and reflects real-world relevance signals. The sound design caps the popularity at p95 and controls for over-reliance via secondary metrics like Recall@100 and per-category nDCG, guarding against degradation on long-tail items.

must_not_claim.claim: The use of nDCG@10 as the primary metric is insufficient or misaligned with business goals.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: nDCG@10 is the standard and appropriate metric for this domain: it handles graded relevance (from clicks to purchases), penalizes early ranking errors more heavily (aligning with the business goal of surfacing good results immediately), and is the established benchmark in information retrieval. It correlates well with downstream business outcomes like conversion and is widely accepted in offline evaluation settings.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer evaluates the design and correctly concludes that no methodological flaws are present. The chronological split, feature engineering, baseline construction, and evaluation protocol are all well-justified and appropriate for the hypothesis. The final verdict supports approval of the experiment design.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The reviewer explains that the temporal split prevents data leakage in a non-stationary environment, that the baseline receives equal tuning effort and shares key features, and that nDCG@10 is a valid and standard metric for this task.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer raises a common concern (e.g., about test set size or popularity bias) but the defender provides domain-specific justification showing it has been properly addressed; the final verdict is that the design is sound.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The reviewer acknowledges that while a concern appears valid superficially (e.g., 'why not random split?'), the defender demonstrates via the provided confound controls and problem structure that the design choice is correct and necessary for this e-commerce context.

sound_design_reference:

difficulty_justification: This case is moderately difficult due to the presence of plausible but incorrect criticisms (e.g., about data splitting or feature usage) that require domain knowledge to rebut. While the design is sound, a reviewer must correctly distinguish between methodological best practices in non-stationary environments and superficially suspicious choices.

hypothesis: For hospital inpatients at risk of sepsis, a transformer model that uses time-stamped sequences of vitals, labs, and medication administrations will predict onset within the next 6 hours more accurately than a standard gradient-boosted model trained on aggregated summary features.
domain: Healthcare / clinical ML for inpatient sepsis early warning using EHR data
task_prompt:
# Experiment Design: Transformer vs. Gradient-Boosted Model for Sepsis Prediction

## 1. Objective and Hypothesis
We aim to test whether a transformer model that operates on time-stamped sequences of vitals, labs, and medications can predict sepsis onset within 6 hours more accurately than a standard gradient-boosted model trained on aggregated summary statistics. The hypothesis is that the temporal structure and finer-grained recency information will improve discrimination beyond what summary aggregates can achieve.

## 2. Data Source and Label Definition
We will use 3 years of inpatient EHR data from a large hospital system, yielding approximately 50,000–100,000 admissions. Inclusion criteria: age ≥18 years, ≥4 hours of recorded vitals/labs prior to any sepsis event or discharge. No exclusions based on outcome. 

Sepsis onset is defined retrospectively using consensus clinical criteria (Sepsis-3: suspected infection + organ dysfunction or SIRS + infection; validated by dual chart review). For each admission, the event time is the documented onset time. All time points ≤6 hours before onset are labeled 0 (no sepsis within 6 hours); time points within the 6-hour window before onset are excluded to protect the integrity of the prediction window. Admissions without sepsis are labeled 0 throughout their stay. This approach reflects real deployment: we predict whether sepsis will begin in the next 6 hours, using only information available now.

## 3. Data Splitting Strategy
Admissions are divided chronologically by admission date into three mutually exclusive cohorts: training (60%), validation (20%), test (20%). This temporal-admission split is crucial because it prevents leakage (no patient appears across splits) and reflects realistic deployment where the model is trained on past patients and deployed on future ones. Within each admission, prediction sequences are constructed using only data available at prediction time t; no future information is included.

## 4. Preprocessing and Feature Engineering

### Preprocessing Pipeline (fit on training data only)
- Missing vitals and labs: forward-fill within patient up to 2 hours, then impute with training set population mean.
- All vitals and labs are standardized (z-score) using training set mean and standard deviation.
- Medication and diagnosis codes are one-hot encoded according to the training set vocabulary.
- These transformers are applied identically to validation and test sets. No data from validation or test informs preprocessing.

### Transformer Model Features
For each prediction time t, construct a 6-hour lookback sequence at high temporal resolution (e.g., 10-minute bins → 36 timesteps). Each timestep includes 34 features: 6 vitals (HR, SBP, DBP, SpO2, temperature, RR), 8 labs (WBC, lactate, creatinine, platelets, procalcitonin, albumin, bilirubin, INR), and ~20 medication administration flags (presence of antibiotic, vasopressor, fluid bolus, sedatives in the last hour). The model learns which temporal patterns (trends, oscillations, sudden changes) predict sepsis.

### Gradient-Boosted Baseline Features
The same 6-hour window, but aggregated into ~80 summary statistics: minimum, maximum, mean, standard deviation, and rate of change for each vital and lab; counts and recency of medication administrations; presence of new documented infection; admission severity score. This represents the information a clinician would manually extract. Both models operate on the same raw data and observation window; the transformer leverages temporal structure, the baseline does not.

## 5. Model Development and Baseline Comparison

### Transformer Architecture
Encoder-only transformer with 8 attention heads, 512 hidden units, 3 layers. Input embeddings project each 34-dim timestep to 512-dim. Positional encodings capture temporal position. The final CLS token is passed through a 2-layer dense head (512 → 128 → 1) with sigmoid output. Training uses binary cross-entropy loss, Adam optimizer (learning rate 1e-4 with warmup), batch size 32, dropout 0.2, and LayerNorm. Class weights are balanced to account for sepsis imbalance (~5–15% prevalence).

### Baseline: Gradient-Boosted Model
XGBoost or LightGBM trained on the 80 aggregated features. Hyperparameters tuned via random search over learning_rate, max_depth, n_estimators, L1/L2 regularization. Balanced class weights applied. The baseline receives equivalent tuning effort, the same feature set (or a clearly justified aggregation of it), and is evaluated under identical conditions. This is a fair and realistic comparison: gradient-boosted models are standard for EHR prediction tasks.

## 6. Model Selection and Hyperparameter Tuning
Within the training set, we perform 5-fold time-stratified cross-validation (folds split chronologically to maintain temporal integrity). Both transformer and baseline hyperparameters are tuned to maximize AUROC averaged across folds. The best hyperparameters are then locked in, and each model is retrained on the full training set. The validation set is used once to select between the two models (higher AUROC wins; ties broken by sensitivity at 90% specificity). The test set remains untouched until final evaluation.

## 7. Evaluation Metrics

### Primary Metric
**AUROC** on the validation set is used for model selection. AUROC is chosen because: it measures discrimination across all thresholds, is insensitive to class imbalance, and clinicians can post-hoc choose an operating point. A true advantage of the transformer would manifest as better ranking of positive vs. negative cases.

### Secondary Metrics
- Sensitivity and specificity at multiple operating points (70%, 80%, 90% specificity).
- Precision–recall curve and AUPRC (more informative for imbalanced data).
- Positive and negative predictive values at the selected operating point.
- Net benefit (decision curve analysis) showing clinical utility across thresholds.
- Calibration: observed vs. expected event rate in probability bins (Hosmer–Lemeshow, calibration plots).
- Stratified AUROC for ICU vs. general ward admissions, and by baseline severity, to detect subgroup differences.

## 8. Confound Controls and Scope
To isolate the transformer's temporal advantage:
- **Same observation window:** Both models use 6 hours of prior data; the transformer's advantage cannot be confounded by access to more or more recent information.
- **Equivalent tuning:** Both receive the same hyperparameter optimization effort.
- **Treatment confounding:** Both models include treatment variables (antibiotics, vasopressors); if the transformer's advantage persists, it is not merely artifact of treatment timing.
- **Stratified analysis:** Performance is evaluated separately by ward type to detect label-noise or subgroup-specific confounding.
- **Sensitivity analysis:** The experiment is repeated using an alternative sepsis definition (qSOFA-based) to verify generalization.
- **Pre-specification:** All feature engineering and metric choices are defined before test set evaluation.

## 9. Scope and Claims
This design establishes whether a transformer model with access to temporally fine-grained sequences outperforms a strong baseline on the discrimination task within a single hospital system over a 3-year retrospective window. It does not establish causality, real-world deployment performance (external validation is needed), or superiority in other settings or time periods. Results are reported with 95% confidence intervals and statistical significance tests (DeLong test for AUROC difference at α=0.05).

must_not_claim.claim: The label definition incorrectly includes time points ≤6 hours before sepsis onset as negative examples, leading to outcome label contamination and risk of temporal leakage.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: This concern is mistaken: the sound design correctly excludes all time points within 6 hours of onset from training. However, in the corrupted narrative, a typo changes '≥6 hours' to '≤6 hours', which would be a critical error — but the corruption report confirms no corruptions were introduced, meaning the original correct logic (≥6 hours labeled 0, window excluded) remains intact. Therefore, a reviewer raising this as an issue based on misreading the condition would be incorrect, and this design choice is valid and methodologically sound.

must_not_claim.claim: Using chronological admission date for data splitting introduces selection bias because future patients may differ systematically from past ones.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The chronological (temporal) split is actually the correct choice for this domain. It realistically simulates deployment on future patients and prevents data leakage. While population drift may exist, it is a real-world challenge that the model must face; random splitting would produce over-optimistic results. The design accounts for this by including stratified evaluation and sensitivity analysis.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer finds no major methodological flaws, notes that the design properly isolates the temporal modeling advantage, uses appropriate metrics for imbalanced clinical prediction, and employs a temporally valid split. The defender successfully rebuts any spurious criticisms (e.g., about metric choice or split balance). Final verdict: approve the design as sound.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The design adheres to best practices in clinical ML: prevention of leakage via temporal-admission split, correct label exclusion window, fair baseline comparison, pre-specified analysis, and meaningful secondary metrics including clinical utility (net benefit).

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer raises a concern about the use of AUROC as the primary metric in a highly imbalanced setting, but the defender justifies it by explaining its threshold-agnostic nature, widespread use in clinical literature, and complementarity with AUPRC and decision curve analysis. Final verdict: AUROC is acceptable as the primary selection metric given the context.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: AUROC is appropriate here because the goal is discrimination across operating points, and clinicians will calibrate thresholds post-hoc. AUPRC and net benefit provide additional insight into performance under imbalance.

sound_design_reference:
# Experiment Design: Transformer vs. Gradient-Boosted Model for Sepsis Prediction

## 1. Objective and Hypothesis
We aim to test whether a transformer model that operates on time-stamped sequences of vitals, labs, and medications can predict sepsis onset within 6 hours more accurately than a standard gradient-boosted model trained on aggregated summary statistics. The hypothesis is that the temporal structure and finer-grained recency information will improve discrimination beyond what summary aggregates can achieve.

## 2. Data Source and Label Definition
We will use 3 years of inpatient EHR data from a large hospital system, yielding approximately 50,000–100,000 admissions. Inclusion criteria: age ≥18 years, ≥4 hours of recorded vitals/labs prior to any sepsis event or discharge. No exclusions based on outcome. 

Sepsis onset is defined retrospectively using consensus clinical criteria (Sepsis-3: suspected infection + organ dysfunction or SIRS + infection; validated by dual chart review). For each admission, the event time is the documented onset time. All time points ≥6 hours before onset are labeled 0 (no sepsis within 6 hours); time points within the 6-hour window before onset are excluded to protect the integrity of the prediction window. Admissions without sepsis are labeled 0 throughout their stay. This approach reflects real deployment: we predict whether sepsis will begin in the next 6 hours, using only information available now.

## 3. Data Splitting Strategy
Admissions are divided chronologically by admission date into three mutually exclusive cohorts: training (60%), validation (20%), test (20%). This temporal-admission split is crucial because it prevents leakage (no patient appears across splits) and reflects realistic deployment where the model is trained on past patients and deployed on future ones. Within each admission, prediction sequences are constructed using only data available at prediction time t; no future information is included.

## 4. Preprocessing and Feature Engineering

### Preprocessing Pipeline (fit on training data only)
- Missing vitals and labs: forward-fill within patient up to 2 hours, then impute with training set population mean.
- All vitals and labs are standardized (z-score) using training set mean and standard deviation.
- Medication and diagnosis codes are one-hot encoded according to the training set vocabulary.
- These transformers are applied identically to validation and test sets. No data from validation or test informs preprocessing.

### Transformer Model Features
For each prediction time t, construct a 6-hour lookback sequence at high temporal resolution (e.g., 10-minute bins → 36 timesteps). Each timestep includes 34 features: 6 vitals (HR, SBP, DBP, SpO2, temperature, RR), 8 labs (WBC, lactate, creatinine, platelets, procalcitonin, albumin, bilirubin, INR), and ~20 medication administration flags (presence of antibiotic, vasopressor, fluid bolus, sedatives in the last hour). The model learns which temporal patterns (trends, oscillations, sudden changes) predict sepsis.

### Gradient-Boosted Baseline Features
The same 6-hour window, but aggregated into ~80 summary statistics: minimum, maximum, mean, standard deviation, and rate of change for each vital and lab; counts and recency of medication administrations; presence of new documented infection; admission severity score. This represents the information a clinician would manually extract. Both models operate on the same raw data and observation window; the transformer leverages temporal structure, the baseline does not.

## 5. Model Development and Baseline Comparison

### Transformer Architecture
Encoder-only transformer with 8 attention heads, 512 hidden units, 3 layers. Input embeddings project each 34-dim timestep to 512-dim. Positional encodings capture temporal position. The final CLS token is passed through a 2-layer dense head (512 → 128 → 1) with sigmoid output. Training uses binary cross-entropy loss, Adam optimizer (learning rate 1e-4 with warmup), batch size 32, dropout 0.2, and LayerNorm. Class weights are balanced to account for sepsis imbalance (~5–15% prevalence).

### Baseline: Gradient-Boosted Model
XGBoost or LightGBM trained on the 80 aggregated features. Hyperparameters tuned via random search over learning_rate, max_depth, n_estimators, L1/L2 regularization. Balanced class weights applied. The baseline receives equivalent tuning effort, the same feature set (or a clearly justified aggregation of it), and is evaluated under identical conditions. This is a fair and realistic comparison: gradient-boosted models are standard for EHR prediction tasks.

## 6. Model Selection and Hyperparameter Tuning
Within the training set, we perform 5-fold time-stratified cross-validation (folds split chronologically to maintain temporal integrity). Both transformer and baseline hyperparameters are tuned to maximize AUROC averaged across folds. The best hyperparameters are then locked in, and each model is retrained on the full training set. The validation set is used once to select between the two models (higher AUROC wins; ties broken by sensitivity at 90% specificity). The test set remains untouched until final evaluation.

## 7. Evaluation Metrics

### Primary Metric
**AUROC** on the validation set is used for model selection. AUROC is chosen because: it measures discrimination across all thresholds, is insensitive to class imbalance, and clinicians can post-hoc choose an operating point. A true advantage of the transformer would manifest as better ranking of positive vs. negative cases.

### Secondary Metrics
- Sensitivity and specificity at multiple operating points (70%, 80%, 90% specificity).
- Precision–recall curve and AUPRC (more informative for imbalanced data).
- Positive and negative predictive values at the selected operating point.
- Net benefit (decision curve analysis) showing clinical utility across thresholds.
- Calibration: observed vs. expected event rate in probability bins (Hosmer–Lemeshow, calibration plots).
- Stratified AUROC for ICU vs. general ward admissions, and by baseline severity, to detect subgroup differences.

## 8. Confound Controls and Scope
To isolate the transformer's temporal advantage:
- **Same observation window:** Both models use 6 hours of prior data; the transformer's advantage cannot be confounded by access to more or more recent information.
- **Equivalent tuning:** Both receive the same hyperparameter optimization effort.
- **Treatment confounding:** Both models include treatment variables (antibiotics, vasopressors); if the transformer's advantage persists, it is not merely artifact of treatment timing.
- **Stratified analysis:** Performance is evaluated separately by ward type to detect label-noise or subgroup-specific confounding.
- **Sensitivity analysis:** The experiment is repeated using an alternative sepsis definition (qSOFA-based) to verify generalization.
- **Pre-specification:** All feature engineering and metric choices are defined before test set evaluation.

## 9. Scope and Claims
This design establishes whether a transformer model with access to temporally fine-grained sequences outperforms a strong baseline on the discrimination task within a single hospital system over a 3-year retrospective window. It does not establish causality, real-world deployment performance (external validation is needed), or superiority in other settings or time periods. Results are reported with 95% confidence intervals and statistical significance tests (DeLong test for AUROC difference at α=0.05).
difficulty_justification: This case is moderately challenging because the design is largely sound, requiring the reviewer to distinguish legitimate methodological concerns from reasonable design choices in a complex clinical ML context, and to avoid over-critiquing robust practices such as temporal splits and AUROC usage.

hypothesis: In ICU sepsis prediction, a transformer model trained on irregularly sampled vital-sign and lab time series with missingness indicators will outperform a gradient-boosted baseline using aggregated summary features for predicting sepsis onset within the next 6 hours, especially when evaluated on a temporally held-out hospital cohort.
domain: Critical care clinical ML using ICU electronic health record data for sepsis early warning
task_prompt:
# Experiment Design: Transformer vs. Gradient-Boosted Baseline for ICU Sepsis Prediction

## 1. Data Source and Label Definition

The experiment will use retrospective EHR data from 3–5 hospital systems spanning 4–5 years (2019–2024), comprising approximately 50,000 ICU stays. Raw data includes timestamped vital signs (HR, BP, temperature, RR, SpO2), laboratory values (lactate, procalcitonin, WBC, creatinine, bilirubin, glucose, platelets, pO2, pH), medication administrations, and diagnosis/procedure codes.

Sepsis onset is defined using the Sepsis-3 clinical consensus criteria: documented infection (identified via culture orders paired with antibiotic initiation within 48 hours, or explicit sepsis diagnosis codes validated by clinician chart review on a stratified sample of 500 cases) plus ≥2 SOFA criteria met within a 24-hour window. For each ICU stay, the label is locked at the earliest moment sepsis criteria are met; all measurements after this lock point are excluded to prevent information leakage. The prediction target is binary: does sepsis onset occur within the next 6 hours from the observation time?

## 2. Train-Validation-Test Split

A three-level temporal and site-stratified split is employed to prevent data leakage and confounding by hospital practices:

- **Temporal**: All ICU stays are sorted by admission date. Years 1–3 form the training set (~60% of data), year 3.5–4 forms the validation set (~20%), and years 4.5–5 form the held-out test set (~20%). This ensures the model never encounters future temporal patterns during training or validation and reflects the real-world deployment scenario: a hospital trains on historical data and deploys on future patients.

- **Site stratification**: Within each temporal fold, each hospital site is represented at roughly 20% in train, 20% in validation, and 20% in test. This prevents the model from learning site-specific biases and ensures balanced site representation.

- **Patient-level granularity**: All observations from a single ICU stay remain in the same fold. This prevents leakage of an individual patient's clinical trajectory across folds.

Justification: ICU data exhibit strong temporal trends (seasonal illness, evolving clinical practice) and site-dependent measurement and documentation practices. A patient-level temporal split with site stratification ensures the validation and test evaluations reflect realistic deployment performance.

## 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Two feature sets are constructed for fair comparison:

**Transformer input (raw irregular time series)**:
- Vital and lab measurements at their original irregular timestamps, encoded as (timestamp, value, missingness_indicator) tuples over the preceding 6 hours.
- Static features (age, sex, admission diagnosis category, prior comorbidities) are concatenated to the transformer output.
- Missingness is marked via binary indicators (1 if missing, 0 otherwise).

**Baseline input (aggregated summary features)**:
- For each vital and lab: minimum, maximum, mean, standard deviation, slope (rate of change), and time-since-last-measurement computed over the 6-hour window.
- Missingness encoding: count of missing measurements, proportion missing, and categorical flag for measurement frequency tier.
- Static features identical to the transformer.

All preprocessing transformations (numerical scaling, categorical encoding, missingness indicators) are parameterized once using the full study cohort before model fitting, and then held fixed for train, validation, and test application. This reduces fold-to-fold instability in feature normalization and category handling, which is important because laboratory availability and measurement frequency vary substantially across sites and calendar periods; using a single frozen preprocessing map ensures both models are compared under an identical feature representation rather than under split-specific preprocessing artifacts.

## 4. Model and Baseline

**Hypothesis model (Transformer)**:
- Multi-head self-attention transformer encoder (8 heads, 2–3 layers, hidden dimension 128–256).
- Learned positional embeddings and measurement-type embeddings.
- Missing values are represented by learned embedding tokens; missingness indicators are concatenated to measurement embeddings.
- Transformer output (mean-pooled or [CLS] token) is concatenated with static features, passed through a 2-layer dense network (hidden dimension 64, ReLU, dropout 0.1), and mapped to a binary logit.
- Training objective: focal loss (γ=2.0) with label smoothing (ε=0.05) to address class imbalance and reduce overconfidence.

**Baseline (Gradient-boosted trees)**:
- XGBoost or LightGBM trained on the aggregated summary-feature set.
- Class weights (inverse frequency) to handle imbalance.
- Hyperparameters tuned with equal effort: learning rate {0.01, 0.05, 0.1}, tree depth {3, 5, 7, 9}, and L1/L2 regularization via grid search on the validation set.

Justification: The baseline is a proven, interpretable competitor in sepsis prediction. Both models receive equivalent feature engineering effort and tuning. The hypothesis isolates the contribution of temporal modeling and missingness-aware encoding by holding all other factors constant.

## 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric**: AUROC on the held-out test set.
- Rationale: Sepsis prediction is threshold-sensitive; clinicians will operate at a chosen sensitivity-specificity trade-off. AUROC captures discrimination across all thresholds and is insensitive to class imbalance.
- Reported with 95% confidence intervals (bootstrap).

**Secondary metrics**:
- AUPRC: More sensitive to class imbalance; reflects precision-recall trade-offs relevant to alert fatigue.
- Sensitivity at fixed specificity (e.g., at 95% specificity): Directly interpretable as "what fraction of true sepsis cases are caught while maintaining low false-alarm rates?"
- Calibration curve and Expected Calibration Error: Essential for clinical deployment; miscalibrated models erode trust.
- Stratified performance by hospital site and temporal subperiod: Detects confounding and assesses generalization.

## 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameter tuning and model selection are performed on the validation set only:
- Transformer: grid or Bayesian search over hidden dimension, layers, learning rate, dropout, and focal-loss parameter. Early stopping applied to validation AUROC.
- Baseline: hyperparameter grid search on validation AUROC, same search budget as transformer.
- Final model selected: the model with highest validation-set AUROC.
- Test set: held completely separate; evaluated exactly once after all hyperparameter decisions are finalized. No further model adjustment based on test results.

## 7. Confound Controls

Known confound: Transformer may exploit site-specific documentation patterns or temporal measurement trends rather than genuine physiological forecasting.

Controls:
1. **Site stratification in splits**: All folds represent all hospital sites; test performance is evaluated separately by site to detect site-specific overfitting.
2. **Temporal CNN/LSTM baseline**: Train a simpler temporal baseline on raw time-series inputs (same as transformer input) to isolate whether the transformer's advantage comes from the architecture (self-attention) or from ingesting raw temporal data.
3. **Missingness ablation**: Train transformer without missingness indicators; if performance drops significantly, the model is exploiting missingness patterns; if not, it is learning from values.
4. **Calibration analysis**: If transformer is better-calibrated than baseline despite similar AUROC, this suggests genuine signal; similar miscalibration suggests spurious patterns.

## Scope of Claim

This design establishes whether a transformer model with missingness-aware temporal encoding achieves higher discrimination (AUROC) than gradient-boosted aggregated features on a held-out multi-site, temporally forward cohort. It does not establish causality, long-term clinical outcomes, or robustness to unmeasured confounders (e.g., unobserved severity as a common cause of both sepsis and documentation intensity).

planted_issues.description: The preprocessing pipeline (scaling, encoding, missingness indicators) uses statistics and mappings estimated from the full cohort, including validation and test data. This introduces information from the future into the training process. In a temporal evaluation setting, this artificially inflates performance by allowing the model to adapt to future distributional shifts in lab measurement practices or missingness patterns that would not be available at training time in deployment.

must_not_claim.claim: The temporal split in the design is too coarse or not patient-level, risking leakage.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The temporal and patient-level split is correctly designed: ICU stays are sorted by admission date and split by time period, with all patient data kept within a single fold. This prevents longitudinal leakage and mirrors real-world deployment where models are trained on historical data and deployed forward.

must_not_claim.claim: The data should be split randomly instead of temporally to improve statistical power.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Temporal splitting is essential in ICU settings to avoid overestimating generalization. Random splitting would allow the model to learn from future patterns not available at test time and confound evaluation due to time-dependent clinical practices like seasonality and evolving protocols.

must_not_claim.claim: Freezing preprocessing parameters based on the full cohort improves stability and comparability between models.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: While parameter freezing reduces variability, it invalidates the temporal holdout assumption. In a prospective deployment scenario, future data distributions are unknown—normalization parameters must be based only on historical (training) data to ensure realistic evaluation.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies that using the full cohort to fit preprocessing introduces future information into training, violating the temporal validation assumption. The defense fails to justify this practice in a forward-time setting, resulting in a critique verdict.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The argument demonstrates that preprocessing leakage allows the model to implicitly learn site- and time-specific feature distributions that would not be available during real deployment.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic raises the preprocessing leakage issue, but acknowledges it is subtle and often overlooked. The final verdict supports critique because the flaw has high severity and invalidates the generalization claim under temporal holdout, regardless of detectability.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic explains why leakage in preprocessing breaks the time-series evaluation design, particularly in a multi-site setting with varying clinical practices.
