hypothesis: Using patient-level longitudinal EHR sequences with prior utilization and lab trends will predict 30-day unplanned hospital readmission better than a static feature baseline built from demographics, diagnosis codes, and last-encounter labs, when evaluated on future admissions from the same health system.
domain: Hospital readmission prediction in a multi-site health system using electronic health records
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Longitudinal EHR Sequences for Readmission Prediction

### 1. Data Strategy

The experiment uses 2–5 years of inpatient discharge records from a multi-site health system EHR warehouse. The population is adult discharges (age ≥18) with complete electronic follow-up data for 30 days post-discharge. Data includes demographics (age, sex, insurance), diagnoses (ICD-10 codes from current and all prior admissions in the lookback window), procedures, medications (orders and administrations), lab results (with timestamps and values), vital signs, encounter-level summaries (LOS, admission source, disposition), and deterministic indicators for readmission status.

The label is binary: unplanned readmission within 30 calendar days of discharge. Unplanned admissions are identified via admission type field or validated against elective procedure schedules. Planned readmissions, patients discharged against medical advice, and inpatient deaths are excluded. This label definition aligns with hospital operations and quality metrics (e.g., CMS readmission penalties).

Expected data quality issues include missing lab values (imputed with domain-specific strategies: critical labs forward-filled, optional labs marked as missing), inconsistent diagnosis coding across sites (mitigated by aggregating to Elixhauser comorbidity categories), and censoring of follow-up data at the observation window boundary (only admissions with complete 30-day follow-up are eligible as index cases).

### 2. Split Strategy: Temporal (Chronological)

A temporal split is mandatory here because readmission is a future event, and the hypothesis emphasizes longitudinal trends. The observation window (months 1–36 or end of data) is divided into three non-overlapping periods:

- **Train (months 1–24):** All eligible discharges in this window become training index cases. All upstream encounter history and lab data is used as features.
- **Validation (months 25–30):** Discharges in this window serve as validation index cases. Features are computed using historical data within each patient's lookback window, but preprocessing parameters (scalers, encoders, imputation) are fit only on training data and applied here.
- **Test (months 31–36 or to end of data):** Final held-out evaluation set. Represents future admissions.

This split prevents information leakage: no training data comes after any validation or test index discharge, and sequence models cannot exploit future utilization patterns. It reflects the deployment setting—the model will be applied to new discharges and will not have access to readmission outcomes at inference time. If the health system operates multiple sites, the test set is stratified by site to ensure representativeness.

### 3. Feature Engineering

**Static baseline features** (for the logistic regression baseline): age at admission, sex, insurance category, Elixhauser comorbidity count and indicators (CHF, COPD, diabetes, renal disease, MI), admission source, discharge disposition (home, SNF, rehab), primary service line, length of stay (hours), count of admissions in prior 6 months, count of admissions in prior 12 months, prior ER visit count. Continuous variables are standardized (zero mean, unit variance fit on training set).

**Longitudinal sequence features** (for the sequence model): For each index discharge, construct a sequence of the patient's last 12 months of encounters (limited to ~20 encounters to manage sequence length). Each encounter is encoded as a vector containing: admission and discharge dates, LOS, service line (one-hot), admission source, disposition, primary and count of secondary diagnoses (concatenated ICD-10 codes or Elixhauser category embeddings), medication orders (aggregated to ATC level 3, one-hot or frequency embedding), lab tests ordered, and admission/discharge vitals (heart rate, blood pressure, temperature). For labs, extract the most recent value of key analytes (creatinine, hemoglobin, glucose, sodium) before discharge, standardized to training-set mean and standard deviation. Encounter sequences are zero-padded to a fixed length. All encodings and scaling are fit on the training set only.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Sequence model:** A recurrent neural network (LSTM or GRU) with attention, or a Transformer encoder. The architecture includes: (1) embedding layer (encodes categorical diagnoses and medications as dense vectors, fit on training data); (2) two to three LSTM/Transformer layers (128–256 hidden units); (3) attention mechanism to weight encounters by relevance; (4) final dense layer with sigmoid output. Class weighting inversely proportional to prevalence is applied during training to address the imbalanced label. Dropout and L2 regularization are applied to prevent overfitting.

**Baseline:** Logistic regression trained on the static feature set. This baseline represents current clinical practice—risk scores based on demographics, comorbidities, and last-encounter information. The baseline receives equal tuning effort: hyperparameter search (L2 regularization strength) is conducted on the validation set using the same metric (AUPRC). Both models are trained on the training set and selected based on validation performance.

This comparison isolates the value of longitudinal sequence information (encounter history, trends) versus static snapshot features.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC) on the test set. AUPRC is chosen because readmission is a relatively low-base-rate outcome, and the operational use case is targeted intervention rather than uniform treatment across all discharges. Case management teams will act on a limited high-risk subset, so the key question is whether the model concentrates true readmissions near the top of the score distribution, where follow-up resources are deployed.

**Secondary metrics:** 
- AUROC to quantify overall discrimination across the full score range.
- Calibration plots and Brier score to ensure predicted probabilities align with observed rates.
- Sensitivity and specificity at clinically relevant thresholds (e.g., threshold for flagging top 20% as high-risk).
- Stratified performance by service line, discharge disposition, and prior admission count to detect subgroup effects and ensure improvements are not driven by confounding.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameters are tuned on the validation set (months 25–30) using AUPRC as the criterion. A grid search explores hidden units, dropout, learning rate, and class weight parameters. The configuration achieving the highest validation AUPRC is locked. The test set is evaluated once, after all modeling decisions are finalized. No test-set data is used during development.

### 7. Controlling for Confounds

The sequence model's gains could stem from legitimate longitudinal risk signals or from exploiting recency bias and unmeasured confounding. Four controls are implemented:

1. **Stratified analysis:** Metrics are reported separately for service lines, discharge dispositions, and subgroups defined by prior admission count and LOS. If gains concentrate in a single stratum, this suggests confounding rather than generalizable signal.

2. **Feature importance inspection:** SHAP or permutation importance is used to examine whether predictions are driven by recent admission intensity (a confounder) or by earlier longitudinal trends (a signal of true risk).

3. **Sensitivity analysis on matched cohorts:** A secondary analysis matches patients on LOS, prior admission count, and service line, then compares sequence model versus logistic regression performance within matched strata. Persistent improvement after matching would strengthen the causal interpretation.

4. **Scope statement:** The experiment tests whether the sequence model discriminates better on the test set, not whether it is causal or clinically effective. Clinical utility would require prospective validation with intervention.

**Expected outcome:** The hypothesis is supported if the sequence model achieves a meaningful improvement in test AUPRC over the static baseline, with consistent gains across key strata and without evidence of recency bias in feature importance.

planted_issues.description: The primary evaluation metric (AUPRC) does not align with the central claim of the hypothesis, which emphasizes overall prediction improvement using longitudinal sequence data. While AUPRC focuses on performance at the top of the risk distribution, the hypothesis and baseline model are framed around overall discrimination, requiring a ranking-based metric like AUROC. Changing the primary metric midstream makes the evaluation sensitive to outcome prevalence and shifts focus from general risk stratification to a narrow top-rank subset without adjusting other design elements accordingly.

must_not_claim.claim: The use of a temporal split (rather than random or grouped cross-validation) introduces unnecessary bias by limiting available data for training and validation.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: A temporal split is methodologically required for this hypothesis because the model must predict future admissions based on past data. Using a random or grouped split would cause temporal leakage, allowing the model to learn from future encounters and inflating performance estimates. Temporal integrity is essential for deployment validity and reflects real-world inference conditions.

must_not_claim.claim: The static feature baseline is too weak because it lacks trend information, making the comparison unfair.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The baseline is correctly designed to reflect current clinical practice—a static risk model using data available at discharge—and ensures the comparison isolates the added value of longitudinal sequences beyond what is already used in practice. Making the baseline too complex would conflate model architecture with feature content.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic correctly identifies that the choice of AUPRC as the primary metric misaligns with the stated hypothesis and undermines the comparative framing of the experiment. A valid defense cannot justify this mismatch, as the hypothesis is not specifically about targeted top-k enrichment but general improvement in readmission prediction.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic shows that the operational goal (targeted intervention on high-risk patients) does not redefine the hypothesis, and that shifting to AUPRC without harmonizing the expected outcome (e.g., specific thresholds, precision goals) or rebalancing the baseline model tuning constitutes methodological inconsistency.

A temporal split is mandatory here because readmission is a future event, and the hypothesis emphasizes longitudinal trends. The observation window (months 1–36 or end of data) is divided into three non-overlapping periods:

- **Train (months 1–24):** All eligible discharges in this window become training index cases. All upstream encounter history and lab data is used as features.
- **Validation (months 25–30):** Discharges in this window serve as validation index cases. Features are computed using historical data within each patient's lookback window, but preprocessing parameters (scalers, encoders, imputation) are fit only on training data and applied here.
- **Test (months 31–36 or to end of data):** Final held-out evaluation set. Represents future admissions.

This split prevents information leakage: no training data comes after any validation or test index discharge, and sequence models cannot exploit future utilization patterns. It reflects the deployment setting—the model will be applied to new discharges and will not have access to readmission outcomes at inference time. If the health system operates multiple sites, the test set is stratified by site to ensure representativeness.

### 3. Feature Engineering

**Static baseline features** (for the logistic regression baseline): age at admission, sex, insurance category, Elixhauser comorbidity count and indicators (CHF, COPD, diabetes, renal disease, MI), admission source, discharge disposition (home, SNF, rehab), primary service line, length of stay (hours), count of admissions in prior 6 months, count of admissions in prior 12 months, prior ER visit count. Continuous variables are standardized (zero mean, unit variance fit on training set).

**Longitudinal sequence features** (for the sequence model): For each index discharge, construct a sequence of the patient's last 12 months of encounters (limited to ~20 encounters to manage sequence length). Each encounter is encoded as a vector containing: admission and discharge dates, LOS, service line (one-hot), admission source, disposition, primary and count of secondary diagnoses (concatenated ICD-10 codes or Elixhauser category embeddings), medication orders (aggregated to ATC level 3, one-hot or frequency embedding), lab tests ordered, and admission/discharge vitals (heart rate, blood pressure, temperature). For labs, extract the most recent value of key analytes (creatinine, hemoglobin, glucose, sodium) before discharge, standardized to training-set mean and standard deviation. Encounter sequences are zero-padded to a fixed length. All encodings and scaling are fit on the training set only.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Sequence model:** A recurrent neural network (LSTM or GRU) with attention, or a Transformer encoder. The architecture includes: (1) embedding layer (encodes categorical diagnoses and medications as dense vectors, fit on training data); (2) two to three LSTM/Transformer layers (128–256 hidden units); (3) attention mechanism to weight encounters by relevance; (4) final dense layer with sigmoid output. Class weighting inversely proportional to prevalence is applied during training to address the imbalanced label. Dropout and L2 regularization are applied to prevent overfitting.

**Baseline:** Logistic regression trained on the static feature set. This baseline represents current clinical practice—risk scores based on demographics, comorbidities, and last-encounter information. The baseline receives equal tuning effort: hyperparameter search (L2 regularization strength) is conducted on the validation set using the same metric (AUROC). Both models are trained on the training set and selected based on validation performance.

This comparison isolates the value of longitudinal sequence information (encounter history, trends) versus static snapshot features.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUROC) on the test set. AUROC is chosen because readmission is an imbalanced outcome (typically 10–20% base rate), and the hospital needs a threshold-independent measure of discrimination to inform triage and resource allocation. AUROC reflects how well the model ranks high-risk patients above low-risk patients, which is the operational goal.

**Secondary metrics:** 
- Precision-Recall AUC (AUPRC) to focus on positive class performance when base rate is low.
- Calibration plots and Brier score to ensure predicted probabilities align with observed rates.
- Sensitivity and specificity at clinically relevant thresholds (e.g., threshold for flagging top 20% as high-risk).
- Stratified performance by service line, discharge disposition, and prior admission count to detect subgroup effects and ensure improvements are not driven by confounding.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameters are tuned on the validation set (months 25–30) using AUROC as the criterion. A grid search explores hidden units, dropout, learning rate, and class weight parameters. The configuration achieving the highest validation AUROC is locked. The test set is evaluated once, after all modeling decisions are finalized. No test-set data is used during development.

### 7. Controlling for Confounds

The sequence model's gains could stem from legitimate longitudinal risk signals or from exploiting recency bias and unmeasured confounding. Four controls are implemented:

1. **Stratified analysis:** Metrics are reported separately for service lines, discharge dispositions, and subgroups defined by prior admission count and LOS. If gains concentrate in a single stratum, this suggests confounding rather than generalizable signal.

2. **Feature importance inspection:** SHAP or permutation importance is used to examine whether predictions are driven by recent admission intensity (a confounder) or by earlier longitudinal trends (a signal of true risk).

3. **Sensitivity analysis on matched cohorts:** A secondary analysis matches patients on LOS, prior admission count, and service line, then compares sequence model versus logistic regression performance within matched strata. Persistent improvement after matching would strengthen the causal interpretation.

4. **Scope statement:** The experiment tests whether the sequence model discriminates better on the test set, not whether it is causal or clinically effective. Clinical utility would require prospective validation with intervention.

**Expected outcome:** The hypothesis is supported if the sequence model achieves ≥5–10% absolute improvement in test AUROC over the static baseline, with consistent gains across key strata and without evidence of recency bias in feature importance.
difficulty_justification: This case is appropriately difficult because the corruption is subtle—AUPRC is a valid and often preferred metric in imbalanced settings—but the misalignment with the hypothesis intent and existing model comparison framework makes it a methodological flaw. Evaluators must recognize that metric choice shapes the interpretation of success and that shifting it without redefining the hypothesis or rebalancing the baseline validation creates a validity gap.

hypothesis: For merchant card-not-present fraud detection, a gradient-boosted model trained on raw transaction sequences and device/merchant graph features will detect more fraudulent transactions at the same false-positive rate than a baseline logistic regression model using only aggregated account-level counts and amounts.
domain: Financial payments fraud detection for a card network or payment processor
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Card-Not-Present Fraud Detection with Sequence Features

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling

The experiment uses 18–24 months of historical card-not-present transactions from the payment processor's production environment. This timeframe provides sufficient volume to train a robust model and allows temporal drift to be observed. Each transaction record includes timestamp (to the second), merchant details, device/browser fingerprint, geolocation, payment instrument metadata, and context from the prior 5–20 transactions for the same card.

Labels are derived from confirmed chargebacks filed by cardholders within 120 days of the transaction, combined with manual fraud investigation outcomes. Transactions without a chargeback or investigation closure within 120 days are labeled legitimate. This definition is conservative—some fraud may go unreported—but avoids speculation and aligns with observable business outcomes. A sensitivity analysis re-runs the experiment with 90-day and 150-day windows to confirm results are robust to label definition.

### 2. Data Splitting Strategy

Data is partitioned using a stratified transaction-level holdout drawn across the 18-month observation window, with approximately 12 months-equivalent volume allocated to training, 3 months-equivalent volume to validation, and 3 months-equivalent volume to the held-out test set. Stratification is performed by fraud label and merchant category so that each split preserves the same mix of rare fraud patterns and vertical-specific risk profiles. This is important because confirmed fraud prevalence can move substantially across seasonal periods; balancing the class and merchant composition across splits makes it easier to attribute differences between the proposed model and baseline to modeling capacity rather than to shifts in case mix.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Two feature sets are constructed:

**Feature Set 1 (Sequence & Graph):** Raw transaction sequences (prior 5, 10, 20 transactions for the same instrument with amounts, merchants, time gaps, device changes, geolocation distance); device/browser change indicators; payment instrument age; merchant category; device co-occurrence patterns; card velocity (transaction count, unique merchant/device counts in prior 7/30/90 days); prior fraud rate.

**Feature Set 2 (Aggregated Account-Level):** Transaction count, total amount, unique merchant count, unique device count, fraud rate in prior 7/30/90 days.

Preprocessing operators are parameterized once before model fitting so that feature representations remain stable across the experiment. Continuous variables use a single set of scaling and imputation parameters estimated from the labeled transaction pool, and categorical vocabularies plus rare-category thresholds are frozen once to avoid split-specific sparsity artifacts. Graph features are constructed from the complete observation window and then materialized as fixed lookup features for training, validation, and test scoring, which keeps merchant/device connectivity estimates comparable across all splits.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Proposed Model:** Gradient-boosted decision tree (XGBoost or LightGBM) trained on Feature Sets 1 + 2 combined. Hyperparameters (max depth, learning rate, n_estimators, L2 regularization) are tuned on the validation set via grid search. Training uses early stopping with a 10% held-out subset of the training set for convergence monitoring.

**Baseline:** Logistic regression trained on Feature Set 2 only (aggregated account-level features). The baseline uses a fixed L2-regularized specification with class weights adjusted for training-set fraud prevalence, without an extensive hyperparameter search. Keeping the linear reference model fixed provides a stable comparator and avoids attributing gains to baseline-specific optimization choices rather than to the additional sequence information. Both models are evaluated under identical conditions on the same test set.

This design ensures that any lift is attributable to raw sequence features and temporal context, not simply access to richer information. A careful critic would verify that the baseline remains a meaningful reference point; using a fixed linear specification keeps that reference interpretable.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary Metric:** PR-AUC. In this setting, fraud thresholds are adjusted operationally as analyst capacity, merchant mix, and seasonal demand change, so the main comparison should emphasize the quality of the score distribution across the full operating range rather than performance at a single intervention point. The hypothesis is supported if the proposed model achieves a materially higher PR-AUC than the baseline on the test set.

**Secondary Metrics:** (1) Recall at 95% precision to quantify performance at a strict review threshold, (2) Recall at 90% and 98% precision to show robustness across operating settings, (3) Recall stratified by merchant category, transaction amount, and account age to detect whether improvements generalize or concentrate in specific segments.

### 6. Model Selection and Validation

Hyperparameters are tuned on the validation set (the validation holdout partition) using the primary metric (PR-AUC). The final model is selected based on validation performance. All operating-threshold summaries reported alongside the primary metric are derived after model selection is finalized, and the test set remains reserved for final reporting only. The test set is used once, at the end, to estimate final comparative performance.

### 7. Controls for Confounds and Scope

To isolate the contribution of sequence modeling, the baseline is given access to the same aggregated device/merchant graph features, ensuring the lift is not merely from richer information. Post-hoc stratified analysis on the test set detects whether improvements are broad or concentrated in narrow segments (e.g., specific merchant categories). Label leakage is mitigated by restricting features to transaction-time and prior behavioral signals, excluding explicit post-transaction outcomes from model inputs.

The experiment establishes whether raw transaction sequences and device/merchant co-occurrence patterns improve fraud detection quality compared to a strong aggregated baseline. It does not establish whether the proposed model generalizes to other payment types (e.g., card-present, digital wallets) or other fraud types (e.g., account takeover); these would require separate evaluation.

planted_issues.description: The design uses a randomly sampled, stratified train/validation/test split across the full 18-month window rather than a chronological split. This allows future transactions to influence the training data, violating the temporal dependency structure of real-world deployment and enabling the model to learn patterns from the future that would not be available at prediction time. This inflates performance unrealistically and invalidates the operational relevance of results.

Preprocessing parameters—including scaling, imputation, categorical encoding, and especially merchant/device co-occurrence graph aggregates—are computed from the entire dataset before splitting. This leaks future transactional information into the training data, as the graph structure and distributional statistics include patterns from validation and test periods, which were not available during the training period. This is particularly harmful in fraud detection, where account behavior and relationships evolve over time.

The logistic regression baseline is not tuned on the validation set and uses a fixed hyperparameter specification. This under-optimizes the baseline, making it less competitive and potentially inflating the perceived gains of the proposed model. A fair comparison requires equal tuning effort for both models to ensure that performance differences reflect architectural capacity, not optimization disparity.

The primary evaluation metric is set to PR-AUC, which measures average performance across all thresholds, while the operational objective is higher recall at a fixed precision (e.g., 95%). A model can improve PR-AUC by performing better at low-precision thresholds irrelevant to practice while failing to improve at the high-precision threshold that governs manual review capacity. This misaligns the evaluation goal with the business impact.

must_not_claim.claim: Stratifying the data split by fraud label and merchant category introduces bias or data leakage.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Stratification by class and category is methodologically sound to balance rare events and vertical-specific risks. The critical issue is not stratification itself, but the use of random sampling across time. A proper design can stratify within chronologically split folds; the flaw is temporal leakage, not stratification.

must_not_claim.claim: Using graph features like merchant co-occurrence patterns is invalid due to instability over time.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Graph features are legitimate and used in production systems, provided they are built exclusively from training-period data. The corruption lies in leaking future data into the graph, not in using graph features per se.

must_not_claim.claim: The sensitivity analysis with 90/150-day label windows is unnecessary or suspicious.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Label sensitivity analysis is a best practice given delayed fraud discovery. The design correctly accounts for partial observability of ground truth and shows robustness across definitions.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies and the defender fails to rebut temporal leakage due to non-chronological splitting. The final verdict concludes that evaluation is invalid without a time-respectful split.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: It is demonstrated that mixing temporal data allows future patterns to inform training, which misaligns with deployment. Correct: chronological test sets only.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic exposes leakage from pooled preprocessing and graph aggregation over the full time window, disqualifying feature stability claims. The defender admits future data influenced training transforms.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Evidence is provided that mean scaling, graph connectivity, or category frequencies depend on post-training data.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic argues that the fixed baseline exaggerates the proposed model's advantage. The defender concedes that unequal tuning inflates gains and commits to re-running with a tuned baseline.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Baseline performance improves meaningfully after tuning, narrowing the gap with the proposed model.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The defender shows that tuning has negligible impact on baseline performance despite effort, supporting use of a fixed spec.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic demonstrates that PR-AUC is misaligned with the goal of catching more fraud at a fixed review capacity. The defender agrees to switch primary metric to recall at 95% precision to reflect operational reality.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: A model can achieve higher PR-AUC but lower recall at 95% precision, showing the metric conflict.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Card-Not-Present Fraud Detection with Sequence Features

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling

The experiment uses 18–24 months of historical card-not-present transactions from the payment processor's production environment. This timeframe provides sufficient volume to train a robust model and allows temporal drift to be observed. Each transaction record includes timestamp (to the second), merchant details, device/browser fingerprint, geolocation, payment instrument metadata, and context from the prior 5–20 transactions for the same card.

Labels are derived from confirmed chargebacks filed by cardholders within 120 days of the transaction, combined with manual fraud investigation outcomes. Transactions without a chargeback or investigation closure within 120 days are labeled legitimate. This definition is conservative—some fraud may go unreported—but avoids speculation and aligns with observable business outcomes. A sensitivity analysis re-runs the experiment with 90-day and 150-day windows to confirm results are robust to label definition.

### 2. Data Splitting Strategy

Data is split chronologically: the first 12 months form the training set, months 13–15 form the validation set, and months 16–18 form the held-out test set. This temporal ordering is essential because (1) it prevents the model from using future information to predict past fraud, (2) it respects the deployment reality where transactions arrive sequentially, and (3) it avoids label leakage from post-transaction fraud signals (chargebacks, investigations) that emerge after the prediction moment. Random shuffling is not performed.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Two feature sets are constructed:

**Feature Set 1 (Sequence & Graph):** Raw transaction sequences (prior 5, 10, 20 transactions for the same instrument with amounts, merchants, time gaps, device changes, geolocation distance); device/browser change indicators; payment instrument age; merchant category; device co-occurrence patterns; card velocity (transaction count, unique merchant/device counts in prior 7/30/90 days); prior fraud rate.

**Feature Set 2 (Aggregated Account-Level):** Transaction count, total amount, unique merchant count, unique device count, fraud rate in prior 7/30/90 days.

All preprocessing transformers (scaling, encoding, imputation, graph aggregation) are fit on the training set only and applied without refitting to validation and test sets. This prevents information leakage. Graph features are computed from the training window and held static during validation/test scoring.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Proposed Model:** Gradient-boosted decision tree (XGBoost or LightGBM) trained on Feature Sets 1 + 2 combined. Hyperparameters (max depth, learning rate, n_estimators, L2 regularization) are tuned on the validation set via grid search. Training uses early stopping with a 10% held-out subset of the training set for convergence monitoring.

**Baseline:** Logistic regression trained on Feature Set 2 only (aggregated account-level features). The baseline receives identical tuning effort: L2 penalty strength is tuned on the validation set via grid search, and class weights are adjusted for training set fraud prevalence. Both models are evaluated under identical conditions on the same test set.

This design ensures that any lift is attributable to raw sequence features and temporal context, not simply access to richer information. A careful critic would verify that the baseline is competitive; tuning both models equally ensures fair comparison.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary Metric:** Recall at fixed precision = 95%. This directly measures the operational objective: detecting more fraud while respecting the review queue capacity (the precision floor prevents flagging everything). The hypothesis is supported if the proposed model achieves ≥5 percentage points higher recall than the baseline at 95% precision on the test set.

**Secondary Metrics:** (1) PR-AUC to summarize performance across thresholds under class imbalance, (2) Recall at 90% and 98% precision to show robustness across review settings, (3) Recall stratified by merchant category, transaction amount, and account age to detect whether improvements generalize or concentrate in specific segments.

### 6. Model Selection and Validation

Hyperparameters are tuned on the validation set (months 13–15) using the primary metric (recall at 95% precision). The final model is selected based on validation performance. No threshold optimization or model selection is performed on the test set; all such decisions are finalized before the test set is evaluated. The test set is held out and touched only once, at the end, to report final performance and avoid overfitting to the evaluation set.

### 7. Controls for Confounds and Scope

To isolate the contribution of sequence modeling, the baseline is given access to the same aggregated device/merchant graph features, ensuring the lift is not merely from richer information. Post-hoc stratified analysis on the test set detects whether improvements are broad or concentrated in narrow segments (e.g., specific merchant categories). Label leakage is prevented by fitting all transformers before the prediction point and not using post-transaction signals from features.

The experiment establishes whether raw transaction sequences and device/merchant co-occurrence patterns improve fraud detection at a fixed precision level, compared to a strong aggregated baseline. It does not establish whether the proposed model generalizes to other payment types (e.g., card-present, digital wallets) or other fraud types (e.g., account takeover); these would require separate evaluation.
difficulty_justification: This case is highly challenging because it combines multiple subtle, interacting corruptions—especially temporal and preprocessing leakage—that mimic robust methodology. Reviewers must disentangle methodologically sound practices (stratification, stability) from temporally invalid applications, and recognize that some corruptions compound in effect, despite individually plausible justifications.

hypothesis: A transformer-based model that encodes the full sequence of prior support tickets and chat messages will predict 14-day customer churn in a B2B SaaS product more accurately than a baseline trained only on aggregate account-level usage metrics, when both are evaluated on future accounts and future time windows.
domain: B2B SaaS customer success analytics
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer-Based Sequence Modeling for 14-Day Customer Churn Prediction

### 1. Objective
Test whether a transformer-based model that encodes full sequences of prior support tickets and chat messages predicts 14-day churn more accurately than a baseline trained only on aggregate account-level metrics, when both are evaluated on future accounts and time windows.

### 2. Data Strategy
The experiment uses 24 months of complete production telemetry from the SaaS environment. Raw data includes:
- Product usage events (daily active users, feature adoption, API call volumes)
- Support tickets (creation/resolution timestamps, category, priority, resolution outcome, text)
- Customer success chat transcripts (timestamps, participant role, message text)
- Subscription events (renewals, cancellations, downgrades, plan changes)
- Billing status (payment failures, account status)

This data spans multiple product releases and seasonal cycles, ensuring the dataset captures diverse account behaviors and is not biased toward a single product phase.

**Label Definition:** An account is labeled as churned (positive class) if it does not renew or explicitly cancels within the 14-day window following the prediction observation date. Downgrades without cancellation are not labeled as churn. Labels are confirmed from subscription database records, not inferred from usage patterns.

### 3. Split Strategy — Temporal with Account-Time Stratification
The dataset is divided into three non-overlapping temporal windows:
- **Training (Months 1–12):** All account-weeks in this window are used for model fitting.
- **Validation (Months 13–18):** Used for hyperparameter tuning, early stopping, and model selection.
- **Test (Months 19–24):** Held out and evaluated once, at the end, to report final metrics.

This temporal ordering is critical: it prevents the model from using future information during training and ensures evaluation reflects the true deployment scenario, where the model scores accounts today and observes churn outcomes 14–28 days in the future. Account IDs are not mixed across splits; if an account appears in the training window, all its historical weeks remain there, preventing data leakage and ensuring the model generalizes to new accounts in future time windows.

### 4. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing
**Aggregate Baseline Features (Set A):** Account age, subscription tenure, plan tier, billing frequency, total support tickets (all-time and 30-day), average ticket resolution time, days since last ticket, days since last login, weekly active user count, cumulative feature adoption count, 90-day payment failure count, stratified churn rate of similar accounts, and an active cancellation-risk flag derived from customer-success workflow state at the observation week.

**Transformer Sequence Features (Set B):** For each account-week observation, construct a chronologically ordered sequence of all support tickets and chat messages from the 90 days prior to the observation date. Each ticket includes: timestamp, resolution timestamp, category, priority, outcome, workflow escalation state, and text (title + description). Each message includes: timestamp, participant role, and text. Sequences are capped at 200 events (tickets + messages combined); padding or truncation is applied uniformly.

Including workflow state alongside the raw interaction stream is intended to preserve high-salience retention signals already present in the operational stack. In practice, escalation markers often summarize nuanced account-health context that would otherwise require the model to infer indirectly from sparse support interactions alone.

**Preprocessing Fit Point:** All preprocessing transformations—feature scaling, imputation, text tokenization, vocabulary, and sequence padding schemas—are fit exclusively on the training window (months 1–12). The fitted transformers and vocabularies are applied without refitting to validation and test sets. This prevents label leakage from validation/test statistics.

### 5. Model and Baseline
**Model:** A transformer-based sequence encoder (e.g., DistilBERT or a multi-head attention stack) takes the chronological sequence of events as input, with positional encodings to capture temporal order. The encoder outputs a pooled representation (e.g., [CLS] token or mean pooling over the sequence), which is passed through a 2-layer feed-forward classifier to produce a churn probability. The model is trained end-to-end with binary cross-entropy loss on the training set.

**Baseline:** Logistic regression trained on the aggregate account-level features (Set A), without sequence information. Hyperparameters for both models (learning rate, number of transformer layers, dropout rate, regularization strength) are tuned via grid search or Bayesian optimization on the validation set using the primary metric (ROC-AUC). Both models receive equivalent tuning effort and computational budget, ensuring fair comparison. The gain of the transformer over the baseline isolates the contribution of sequential modeling.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics
**Primary Metric:** ROC-AUC on the test set (months 19–24). ROC-AUC is chosen because it is threshold-independent (the team can set their own precision-recall tradeoff based on intervention capacity), robust to class imbalance, and directly measures ranking quality—the core operational goal. A 95% confidence interval is computed via bootstrap.

**Secondary Metrics:**
- Precision@K and Recall@K (K = 5%, 10%, 20%): reflect intervention outcomes if the team acts on the top K% of flagged accounts.
- PR-AUC: alternative robustness check for imbalanced data.
- Expected Calibration Error: ensures predicted probabilities reflect true churn frequencies.
- Per-cohort ROC-AUC (stratified by plan tier, customer tenure): checks for generalization across account types.
- Monthly ROC-AUC in test window: detects performance drift over time.

### 7. Model Selection and Test Set Policy
Hyperparameter tuning occurs exclusively on the validation set. Early stopping is triggered based on validation ROC-AUC. The test set is never touched during development and is evaluated exactly once, at the end, using the final trained models. This prevents accidental overfitting to the test set.

### 8. Controls for Confounds
**Event Frequency Baseline:** A secondary baseline (XGBoost or random forest) trained on aggregate features *including explicit event counts and recency signals* tests whether the transformer's gain comes from sequence modeling or merely from richer metadata. If the transformer outperforms this enriched baseline, the improvement comes from learning sequential dependencies.

**Stratified Evaluation:** Separately report ROC-AUC for accounts stratified by plan tier, customer tenure, and industry to ensure the model does not overfit to one segment.

**Ablation Studies:** Re-evaluate the transformer using only ticket sequences or only chat sequences to isolate which signal drives performance.

**Temporal Consistency:** Report month-by-month performance in the test window to detect drift and confirm generalization to future time periods.

### 9. Scope and Interpretation
This design establishes whether the transformer model, given access to full historical sequences of tickets and messages, ranks churned accounts higher than a statistical model trained only on aggregate metrics. It does not establish whether sequence modeling would outperform a different architecture (e.g., an RNN or 1D CNN), nor does it measure the cost-benefit of collecting and preprocessing text data. The confound controls ensure the improvement is not due to the transformer simply having access to more event metadata; it must come from learning temporal dependencies.

planted_issues.description: The inclusion of an 'active cancellation-risk flag' and 'workflow escalation state' as features introduces target leakage. These operational signals are often set *because* an account is about to churn or has already entered a cancellation workflow, meaning they encode information that is downstream of the target variable. A model that uses them is effectively being told the answer, as these flags are proxies for the outcome rather than predictive inputs. This invalidates the experiment's claim to predict churn before it happens.

must_not_claim.claim: The temporal split might not be sufficiently large or balanced, as training uses 12 months and test uses only 6 months.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The duration of split windows does not need to be equal; what matters is chronological order and non-overlap. Here, the 12-month training window ensures stability of patterns, while the 6-month test captures multiple seasonal cycles — both are sufficient for generalization in B2B SaaS settings.

must_not_claim.claim: The transformer model's use of raw text and workflow states together is inappropriate complexity.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Combining interaction history with operational metadata is domain-appropriate in B2B SaaS, as long as the metadata is not derived from the same process leading to churn. The flaw is not in the integration of modalities but in the use of *causal* markers of churn itself.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic correctly identifies that the 'cancellation-risk flag' and 'workflow escalation state' may encode information about churn causation or consequence, and demonstrates that using such features allows the model to exploit shortcuts not available at prediction time. The final verdict is that the experiment is invalid unless these features are removed or proven to be set *before* any churn decision.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Evidence that workflow flags are set conditionally on churn likelihood or cancellation initiation, or that such flags are updated during or after the prediction window.

The model is trained end-to-end with binary cross-entropy loss on the training set.

**Baseline:** Logistic regression trained on the aggregate account-level features (Set A), without sequence information. Hyperparameters for both models (learning rate, number of transformer layers, dropout rate, regularization strength) are tuned via grid search or Bayesian optimization on the validation set using the primary metric (ROC-AUC). Both models receive equivalent tuning effort and computational budget, ensuring fair comparison. The gain of the transformer over the baseline isolates the contribution of sequential modeling.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics
**Primary Metric:** ROC-AUC on the test set (months 19–24). ROC-AUC is chosen because it is threshold-independent (the team can set their own precision-recall tradeoff based on intervention capacity), robust to class imbalance, and directly measures ranking quality—the core operational goal. A 95% confidence interval is computed via bootstrap.

**Secondary Metrics:**
- Precision@K and Recall@K (K = 5%, 10%, 20%): reflect intervention outcomes if the team acts on the top K% of flagged accounts.
- PR-AUC: alternative robustness check for imbalanced data.
- Expected Calibration Error: ensures predicted probabilities reflect true churn frequencies.
- Per-cohort ROC-AUC (stratified by plan tier, customer tenure): checks for generalization across account types.
- Monthly ROC-AUC in test window: detects performance drift over time.

### 7. Model Selection and Test Set Policy
Hyperparameter tuning occurs exclusively on the validation set. Early stopping is triggered based on validation ROC-AUC. The test set is never touched during development and is evaluated exactly once, at the end, using the final trained models. This prevents accidental overfitting to the test set.

### 8. Controls for Confounds
**Event Frequency Baseline:** A secondary baseline (XGBoost or random forest) trained on aggregate features *including explicit event counts and recency signals* tests whether the transformer's gain comes from sequence modeling or merely from richer metadata. If the transformer outperforms this enriched baseline, the improvement comes from learning sequential dependencies.

**Stratified Evaluation:** Separately report ROC-AUC for accounts stratified by plan tier, customer tenure, and industry to ensure the model does not overfit to one segment.

**Ablation Studies:** Re-evaluate the transformer using only ticket sequences or only chat sequences to isolate which signal drives performance.

**Temporal Consistency:** Report month-by-month performance in the test window to detect drift and confirm generalization to future time periods.

### 9. Scope and Interpretation
This design establishes whether the transformer model, given access to full historical sequences of tickets and messages, ranks churned accounts higher than a statistical model trained only on aggregate metrics. It does not establish whether sequence modeling would outperform a different architecture (e.g., an RNN or 1D CNN), nor does it measure the cost-benefit of collecting and preprocessing text data. The confound controls ensure the improvement is not due to the transformer simply having access to more event metadata; it must come from learning temporal dependencies.
difficulty_justification: The corruption is subtle because 'workflow escalation state' appears to be a legitimate operational feature and may seem like a reasonable proxy for account health; detecting the issue requires understanding the causal direction between workflow triggers and churn, not just feature inspection.

hypothesis: For enterprise customer support ticket triage, a transformer model fine-tuned on ticket text plus prior interaction history will outperform a baseline bag-of-words classifier on predicting the correct resolution category and escalation path for newly opened tickets.
domain: Enterprise customer support operations using historical ticketing system data and agent-assigned resolution labels

## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. Bag-of-Words for Support Ticket Triage

### Objective
To test whether a fine-tuned transformer model, trained on ticket text and customer interaction history, achieves higher routing accuracy than a bag-of-words baseline when predicting the correct resolution category and escalation path for newly opened support tickets.

### 1. Data Strategy

The experiment uses 24–36 months of closed support tickets from a single enterprise ticketing system. The dataset includes at least 200,000 tickets spanning all resolution categories (technical fixes, billing, escalations, feature requests, etc.). Each ticket contains: subject line, full message thread (customer messages and agent notes), customer metadata (account tier, product area, support channel), and ground-truth agent-assigned resolution category and escalation outcome.

Class imbalance is expected to be severe; ticket distribution and any known category definition changes during the 24-month period are documented upfront. The label is defined as the agent-assigned resolution category at the time of ticket closure—we treat agent decisions as ground truth, reflecting real operational routing decisions.

### 2. Split Strategy

To ensure that train, validation, and test all contain sufficient support for low-frequency routing outcomes, the data is partitioned using stratified allocation by resolution category across the full 24-month pool:

- **Training set:** ~75% of tickets.
- **Validation set:** ~12.5% of tickets.
- **Test set:** ~12.5% of tickets.

This split design is chosen because some categories appear in concentrated bursts associated with releases, incident clusters, or billing-cycle events. Preserving the overall category mix in each fold makes the comparison between the transformer and baseline less sensitive to month-specific case-mix volatility and ensures that rare categories are represented throughout model development. Ticket timing remains available as an input signal through recency-based features, but the partitioning itself is defined to maintain comparable label support across folds.

### 3. Feature Engineering

**Raw signals:**
- Ticket subject line and full message thread (tokenized via pre-trained transformer vocabulary).
- Customer metadata: account tier, product area, support channel (one-hot or embedded).
- Interaction history: prior ticket count (30/90/365-day windows), average past resolution time, escalation rate on prior tickets.

**Preprocessing pipeline:**
- Tokenizer vocabulary, categorical level maps, imputation defaults, and numeric scaling constants are fixed once from the study corpus and then reused unchanged for all folds.
- Validation and test sets are transformed using this frozen preprocessing specification so that feature definitions remain identical across model development and final evaluation.
- All interaction history features are computed with a cutoff at ticket open time to avoid look-ahead bias.

**Leakage prevention:**
- Ticket threads are audited to exclude post-hoc agent notes written long after routing (e.g., >2 hours after ticket open). If this is infeasible, only the customer-written portion of the thread is used for model input.
- Learned attention weights are inspected post-hoc to verify the model is not relying on late-stage resolution language.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Model:** A transformer encoder (e.g., BERT, RoBERTa, or DistilBERT), fine-tuned on ticket text, pooled via [CLS] token or mean pooling, concatenated with customer metadata embeddings and interaction history features, and passed through a 2–3 layer dense head (256–512 hidden units, ReLU, dropout 0.1–0.2) to a softmax output over resolution categories. Trained end-to-end with categorical cross-entropy loss, optionally weighted by inverse class frequency.

**Baseline:** Logistic regression with bag-of-words (TF-IDF) features derived from subject + thread text (unigrams + bigrams, min document frequency 5, max features 5000), concatenated with the identical customer metadata and interaction history features used by the transformer.

**Comparison framing:** Both models receive the same feature inputs and are evaluated under the same split and reporting protocol. The baseline uses a fixed regularization setting with balanced class weights so it serves as a stable linear reference, while the transformer receives fuller hyperparameter search because its larger hypothesis space makes optimization choices materially affect performance. The resulting comparison tests whether contextual sequence modeling yields a measurable advantage over a strong lexical model under the same operational feature view.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Macro-averaged F1 score (F1_macro). This metric is chosen because: (1) the business goal is accurate routing for all ticket types, including rare categories; (2) macro-F1 penalizes poor performance on minority classes, which is critical for operational fairness; (3) F1 captures the precision-recall tradeoff, important for threshold-sensitive routing logic.

**Secondary metrics:**
- Weighted F1 (accounts for category volume in operations).
- Per-category precision, recall, and F1 (diagnostic: identify which categories improve).
- Top-1 accuracy (reference metric).
- Expected calibration error (ECE; important if downstream routing uses confidence thresholds).
- Binary escalation prediction accuracy (secondary task, operationally critical).

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameter tuning for the transformer is performed on the validation set using grid or random search over learning rate, batch size, epochs, regularization strength, dropout, and dense head depth. Early stopping is applied during training using validation macro-F1. The model checkpoint with the highest validation macro-F1 is selected.

After this selection step, the held-out test fold is used as a final deployment-readiness check to verify that the chosen configuration preserves its advantage under the fully frozen pipeline. If the observed margin against the baseline is directionally unstable relative to validation, one conservative training-procedure adjustment is allowed (for example, preferring the more stable of two adjacent checkpoints or removing class weighting) before locking the final test-set report. Final performance is reported with 95% confidence intervals via bootstrap.

### 7. Confound Controls

**Post-hoc leakage:** Agent notes written after routing are excluded or time-windowed. Attention weights are audited for reliance on late-stage resolution language.

**Metadata confound:** If customer metadata is not available at real routing time, a sensitivity analysis is run with metadata excluded.

**Policy drift:** Known policy changes are documented. If severe, analysis is restricted to a narrower window or subset of categories.

**Class imbalance:** Stratified partitions and macro-F1 primary metric address imbalance. Sensitivity check: resampling (oversampling, SMOTE) is applied to confirm the transformer advantage is robust.

**Sample size:** Per-category sample counts are reported; categories with <50 test samples are flagged as low-confidence.

### 8. Success Criteria

The hypothesis is confirmed if the transformer model achieves **statistically significantly higher macro-F1 on the test set** compared to the baseline, with the improvement being **operationally meaningful** (e.g., >5% relative improvement in macro-F1) and **robust across rare and common categories** (verified by per-category breakdown and weighted metrics).

planted_issues.description: The design uses a non-temporal, stratified random split over the full 24-month period, which leaks future support policies, customer behavior, and routing practices into training. In a real deployment, models are trained on historical data and predict on future tickets; mixing time periods invalidates this generalization test and risks inflating performance due to temporal alignment.

Preprocessing components (tokenizer vocabulary, categorical encodings, scaling constants) are fitted on the full dataset before splitting, allowing validation and test data to influence the feature engineering pipeline. This contaminates the training process with information from the held-out sets and invalidates the evaluation's independence.

The baseline model receives no hyperparameter tuning and uses a fixed regularization setting, while the transformer undergoes extensive search. This creates an unfair comparison where performance differences may stem from optimization imbalance rather than architectural superiority, undermining the causal interpretation of results.

The test set is used to guide a model adjustment (e.g., selecting a more stable checkpoint or disabling class weighting) if results appear unstable, which violates the principle of a single, final evaluation. Any use of test results to influence model decisions invalidates the held-out nature of the test fold and introduces bias.

must_not_claim.claim: The lack of separate customer-level or session-level leakage handling is a flaw.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Customer-level ticket overlap across folds is expected in production systems—agents handle returning customers—and is acceptable as long as temporal order is preserved. The corrupted design fails to do this, but the presence of such overlap itself is not inherently problematic.

must_not_claim.claim: Using macro-F1 as the primary metric is inappropriate because it ignores operational volume differences across categories.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: While weighted metrics reflect volume, macro-F1 is correctly prioritized here because the business goal requires robust performance on rare but critical tickets (e.g., security escalations), and the hypothesis emphasizes general routing improvement across all categories, not just common ones.

must_not_claim.claim: Including customer metadata and interaction history features gives the model unfair access to non-text signals.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: These features are part of the operational feature set available at routing time in enterprise support systems. Their inclusion is realistic and necessary for a fair test of end-to-end performance; excluding them would misrepresent the deployment scenario.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies and explains at least one of the temporal leakage, preprocessing leakage, broken baseline, or evaluation contamination issues. The final verdict is 'critique' because the design has multiple high-severity flaws that invalidate the fairness and generalizability of the comparison.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic correctly argues that mixing temporal periods leaks future patterns into training, or that preprocessing fit on full data contaminates the pipeline, or that unequal tuning invalidates the baseline, or that test-set-guided adjustments bias evaluation.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies both temporal and preprocessing leakage, noting they compound: because data is shuffled across time, preprocessing leakage becomes harder to detect and more damaging. The verdict acknowledges that even if rare class support is improved via stratification, it does not justify abandoning temporal integrity in a time-evolving domain.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic connects the choice of random stratification with the preprocessing contamination, showing how they jointly invalidate the evaluation.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic accepts the argument that some stability measures are operationally relevant but correctly rejects any adjustment based on test performance, insisting that validation should guide all decisions and test remain untouched. The final report must reflect performance from a single, predefined protocol.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic distinguishes between internal training stability (acceptable) and test-set-informed decisions (unacceptable), and insists on a strict one-time test evaluation.

## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. Bag-of-Words for Support Ticket Triage

### Objective
To test whether a fine-tuned transformer model, trained on ticket text and customer interaction history, achieves higher routing accuracy than a bag-of-words baseline when predicting the correct resolution category and escalation path for newly opened support tickets.

### 1. Data Strategy

The experiment uses 24–36 months of closed support tickets from a single enterprise ticketing system. The dataset includes at least 200,000 tickets spanning all resolution categories (technical fixes, billing, escalations, feature requests, etc.). Each ticket contains: subject line, full message thread (customer messages and agent notes), customer metadata (account tier, product area, support channel), and ground-truth agent-assigned resolution category and escalation outcome.

Class imbalance is expected to be severe; ticket distribution and any known category definition changes during the 24-month period are documented upfront. The label is defined as the agent-assigned resolution category at the time of ticket closure—we treat agent decisions as ground truth, reflecting real operational routing decisions.

### 2. Temporal Split Strategy

To reflect real-world deployment and avoid leakage from future policy changes or agent decisions, the data is split chronologically by ticket open date:

- **Training set:** Tickets opened during months 1–18 (~75% of corpus).
- **Validation set:** Tickets opened during months 19–21 (~12.5% of corpus).
- **Test set:** Tickets opened during months 22–24 (~12.5% of corpus).

Within each fold, tickets are stratified by resolution category to preserve class distribution. This temporal split captures how the model performs on future, unseen support environments and ensures that no information from validation or test periods leaks into training.

### 3. Feature Engineering

**Raw signals:**
- Ticket subject line and full message thread (tokenized via pre-trained transformer vocabulary).
- Customer metadata: account tier, product area, support channel (one-hot or embedded).
- Interaction history: prior ticket count (30/90/365-day windows), average past resolution time, escalation rate on prior tickets.

**Preprocessing pipeline:**
- Text tokenizer and subword vocabulary are fit on training set only.
- Categorical encoders and numeric feature scaling (zero-mean, unit-variance standardization) are fit on training set.
- Validation and test sets are transformed using training-fitted transformers—no re-fitting.
- All interaction history features are computed with a cutoff at ticket open time to avoid look-ahead bias.

**Leakage prevention:**
- Ticket threads are audited to exclude post-hoc agent notes written long after routing (e.g., >2 hours after ticket open). If this is infeasible, only the customer-written portion of the thread is used for model input.
- Learned attention weights are inspected post-hoc to verify the model is not relying on late-stage resolution language.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Model:** A transformer encoder (e.g., BERT, RoBERTa, or DistilBERT), fine-tuned on ticket text, pooled via [CLS] token or mean pooling, concatenated with customer metadata embeddings and interaction history features, and passed through a 2–3 layer dense head (256–512 hidden units, ReLU, dropout 0.1–0.2) to a softmax output over resolution categories. Trained end-to-end with categorical cross-entropy loss, optionally weighted by inverse class frequency.

**Baseline:** Logistic regression with bag-of-words (TF-IDF) features derived from subject + thread text (unigrams + bigrams, min document frequency 5, max features 5000), concatenated with the identical customer metadata and interaction history features used by the transformer.

**Fair comparison:** Both models receive the same feature set, the same preprocessing pipeline (fit on train), and equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort (grid search on validation set). Any performance gap is attributable to the transformer's ability to capture contextual and sequential patterns, not to asymmetric feature engineering or tuning.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Macro-averaged F1 score (F1_macro). This metric is chosen because: (1) the business goal is accurate routing for all ticket types, including rare categories; (2) macro-F1 penalizes poor performance on minority classes, which is critical for operational fairness; (3) F1 captures the precision-recall tradeoff, important for threshold-sensitive routing logic.

**Secondary metrics:**
- Weighted F1 (accounts for category volume in operations).
- Per-category precision, recall, and F1 (diagnostic: identify which categories improve).
- Top-1 accuracy (reference metric).
- Expected calibration error (ECE; important if downstream routing uses confidence thresholds).
- Binary escalation prediction accuracy (secondary task, operationally critical).

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameter tuning is performed on the validation set (months 19–21) using grid or random search over learning rate, batch size, epochs, regularization strength, dropout, and dense head depth. Early stopping is applied during training using validation macro-F1. The model checkpoint with the highest validation macro-F1 is selected. The test set is held out and used only once, after all tuning is complete, to report final performance with 95% confidence intervals (via bootstrap).

### 7. Confound Controls

**Post-hoc leakage:** Agent notes written after routing are excluded or time-windowed. Attention weights are audited for reliance on late-stage language.

**Metadata confound:** If customer metadata is not available at real routing time, a sensitivity analysis is run with metadata excluded.

**Policy drift:** Known policy changes are documented. If severe, analysis is restricted to a narrower window or subset of categories.

**Class imbalance:** Stratified splits and macro-F1 primary metric address imbalance. Sensitivity check: resampling (oversampling, SMOTE) is applied to confirm the transformer advantage is robust.

**Sample size:** Per-category sample counts are reported; categories with <50 test samples are flagged as low-confidence.

### 8. Success Criteria

The hypothesis is confirmed if the transformer model achieves **statistically significantly higher macro-F1 on the test set** compared to the baseline, with the improvement being **operationally meaningful** (e.g., >5% relative improvement in macro-F1) and **robust across rare and common categories** (verified by per-category breakdown and weighted metrics).
difficulty_justification: This case is highly challenging due to multiple interacting corruptions—particularly the combination of temporal and preprocessing leakage—that make flaws harder to isolate. Additionally, some corrupted justifications sound plausible (e.g., stabilizing rare categories, ensuring feature consistency), requiring evaluators to distinguish valid methodological trade-offs from fundamental violations of evaluation integrity.

hypothesis: A temporal graph neural network trained on customer–merchant–device interaction sequences will detect first-party payment fraud more accurately than a gradient-boosted model on aggregated account-level features, especially for newly observed fraud rings, when evaluated on future transactions.
domain: Card payments fraud detection in a large online payments platform
